text:
The generalists completely misjudged $TRT looking at it through the lens of a legacy semiconductor testing company.

They missed the absolute mechanical reality that you cannot scale high-performance computing without cross-stack reliability testing, and the data layer shows all four pillars are now fully running.
The thesis hitting the tape proves exactly why the runway toward a $1B valuation has so much structural momentum:

Memory & Compute Convergence: Their back-end semiconductor solutions segment just hit a massive 124% YoY explosion. Securing multi-million dollar follow-on orders for specialized burn-in boards backstopping next-generation AI GPU platforms proves they are an un-bypassable gatekeeper for the core compute and HBM stack.  

The Power & Photonics Layer: The crowd is totally blind to their dynamic tester system shipments for Silicon Carbide (SiC) and Gallium Nitride (GaN) power modules. As advanced hardware environments transition from copper to light, the structural heat and signal redlining mean automated power testing and optical co-packaging reliability are mandatory toll-booths, not optional choices.  

They are aggressively expanding their physical footprint with a massive new 104,000-square-foot facility in Malaysia just to absorb the sheer velocity of AI hardware testing demand.  

The tourists are still trying to figure out if the momentum is real, while the smart money is just watching the fundamental checklist get ticked off one by one. Still incredibly early in the infrastructure cycle.  

Long the cross-stack bottlenecks.

text:
The market cap disparity here is where the absolute percentage torque is hiding.

While generalists chase the massive liquidity pool of a larger cap play like $AAOI, they completely miss the structural math of relative scaling. Owning both is the ultimate play on the broader optical super-cycle, but ignoring the sheer headroom on Sivers ($SIVE) at these valuation levels is a massive blind spot.

The structural reality between the two tells you exactly where the asymmetric upside sits:

The Valuation Ceiling: $AAOI is an exceptional, vertically integrated photonics asset, but a larger market cap means moving the needle 2x or 3x takes an astronomical amount of capital. $SIVE sitting at a fraction of that size means any major US hyperscaler contract win translates into immediate, hyper-violent torque on the stock price.

The Transatlantic Re-Rate: The market continues to discount Sivers because of its European listing heritage, failing to see the structural pipeline of its ongoing US flip. As it completes the transition into a fully US-domiciled entity, it systematically opens the floodgates for domestic small-cap growth funds that are contractually prohibited from buying foreign listings.
 
The 100 SEK Threshold: Complaining about a past 2,000% run is classic rear-view mirror trading. Crossing the 100 SEK ($10) mark didn't signal the end of the move, it cleared the institutional liquidity mandates that kept the largest desks from accumulating the float.

You don't have to pick a single winner when the entire copper-to-light paradigm shift is choking out global capacity. But if you are hunting for raw, high-torque percentage expansion as the independent Western chokepoint gets priced in, the path of least resistance is incredibly clear.

Long both, but $AAOI is my personal favorite ! 📈

Traditional electronics move data using electricity through copper wires. Photonics moves data using light through fiber-optic cables. Light can carry much more information and travel much farther than electrical signals. ⸻ What is a transceiver? A transceiver is a small device that sits at each end of a fiber-optic cable. Its job is to: Take electrical data from a server or switch. -Convert it into flashes of light. -Send that light through fiber. -Receive light coming back. -Convert it back into electrical data. Think of it as a translator between electronics and light. ⸻ Where does $AAOI fit in? $AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics) manufactures: -Optical transceivers -Lasers -Fiber-optic networking components Their products are used inside: AI data centers Cloud providers Telecom networks Broadband networks ⸻ Why AI is making this important A modern AI data center may contain: Thousands of GPUs Massive amounts of data moving between them The GPUs can’t work together efficiently unless they can communicate extremely fast. That’s where optical networking comes in. The data path looks like: GPU → Switch → Transceiver → Fiber → Transceiver → Switch → GPU Every connection requires optical hardware."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2067444958332584107", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "37779", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2068110479390855576", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10234", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — June 19, 2026

📊 SMH: $659.88 (+5.76%)

📈 Earnings:
• No new semiconductor earnings released today.

🔥 Major Developments:
• China’s AI-infrastructure roadmap keeps broadening: a proposed ~$295B five-year data-center buildout is pulling domestic optics, InP substrates, EMLs, CW lasers, silicon photonics, and optical engines deeper into the strategic supply chain.
• Rubin Ultra’s memory stack debate shifted toward 12-hi HBM near term rather than 16-hi, with hybrid-bonding cost/yield still looking too painful for broad HBM insertion before later generations.
• Architect Labs raised $24M for custom chip development, another sign that venture dollars are still hunting for AI silicon alternatives beyond the GPU incumbents.
• John Jumper, the Nobel-winning AlphaFold leader, left Google DeepMind for Anthropic, extending the frontier-lab talent war that ultimately feeds the compute, networking, and power arms race.

text:
Regarding $SIVE vs $AAOI

$AAOI is in photonics and thus well positioned, but it has baggage in its history and 13b market cap.

$SIVEV is in photonics and has a clean history and a 2.8b market cap.

It's easy to see Sivers stands to gain a lot more in percentage terms.

Yes, you can argue American companies grow faster than European, but Sivers is well underway to becoming an American company.

The only people shilling $AAOI and not $SIVE are the people who are holding $AAOI and missed $SIVE's 2000% run and are mad about it.

But if you're thinking of buying either right now $SIVE is the easy choice.

text:
Portfolio Update (Mid-June'26): +161% (YTD)

1. $SIVE (37%) - InP laser supply is not enough to fill demand. Tied to all hyperscaler supply chains & OEMs. 

$JBL, Ayar Labs & $GFS being the most notable confirmation of volume production from H2'26. 

Holding this intact. I'm looking for my next entry now. Said I would keep adding on nice entries until year end. 

Haven't trimmed a single share because the story hasn't even played out yet. We're still early. Long $SIVE! 

2. $NBIS (26.5%) - 4th hyperscaler in the making. Definitely a great long. AI compute demand keeps growing. 

Software moat makes them #1. That's where applications run, at the end of the day. 

Trimmed from 35% to 25%. $400 looks fair by year end but feel like we will have a little slow down. 

Could be wrong too. So wouldn't recommend for longs that don't want to rotate. Tax cuts are also a thing to consider. Long $NBIS! 

3. Cash (14%) - Built a cash position to strategically enter into a new possible huge long position or maybe a couple of small longer positions. Possibly add into my existing ones like $BRUN, $TRT, etc. 

While I did say I was happy with memory exposure, still seems low. Will try to gain more exposure over there. 

4. $BRUN (3.5%) - NVDA exemplar cloud certification is a sign of strong reliability. Why would $DELL CEO just mention them like that? Looks to be the next $CRWV. 

Their revenue growth and projections keep scaling at a level that's consistent and unbelievably good. 

Feel like 2027 will be the year for them. We got some slight short-term share lockup stuff hovering that could provide opportunities to buy but also keep the stock out of radar of the big guns. Long $BRUN!

5. $PENG (6%) - AI server demand growth is so high. I got surprised when $DELL released their earnings. 

$PENG having stronger long-term upside with photonic & memory exposure that could expand margins. Long $PENG! 

6. $TRT (4.5%) - Backend testing and sales of burn-in-boards for testing chips. Growing at triple-digits and a very good balance sheet. 

10x potential as chip's margin for error before release is lower. Demand > supply gap being one of the main reasons. Long $TRT! 

7. Swings & Longs combined (8.5%) - $AAOI, $CRDO, $SMCI, $KEEL

$AAOI, $CRDO are swings that I am looking to keep a few as longs due to huge growth in the coming years. Still feels really early. Photonics story in 2027 is going to be even bigger.

$SMCI & $KEEL are definitely the ones that I won't hold long-term. 

$KEEL will go out when leases confirm with hyperscalers hit on the news and it reaches somewhere around $10+. Maybe $20? Let us see! 

$SMCI is being criticized for the right reasons but I feel they can have a great turnaround and margins will get better. 4x from here. Not the quickest swing. Cheap! 0.5x sales bro. 

One thing I didn't mention earlier was $SMCI was a swing position and not a long position. So I added it to my swing list now. 

Also sold $ASTS since I wanted to fill my cash position again. But feels like a $100 soon. Don't know why street is being irrational here after Bluebird successful launch.

$MU is also a small position in this to ride to $2T+. I want to increase memory exposure, as I mentioned. So I'll be doing that once I can.

text:
土曜朝ニュース（AI光接続）

AIデータセンターの主戦場がGPUだけじゃなく「つなぐ部分」に移ってきた。

銅配線は短距離なら強い。でもラックが巨大化すると電力・発熱・遅延がボトルネックになる。

$NVDA はCPO/シリコンフォトニクスで従来の着脱式光トランシーバ比5倍の電力効率をうたう。Spectrum-X Ethernet Photonicsは409.6Tb/sで2026年後半提供予定。

TowerはNVIDIA向け1.6T光モジュールで協業。MarketsandMarketsはSiPho市場を2024年$2.16Bから2030年$9.65Bへ拡大と予測。

GPUの次は「電力」と「光」。

見ておきたいのは
$LITE  $COHR  $CRDO  $AVGO  $MRVL  $ANET  

レーザー・光モジュール・高速接続がAIインフラの次の争奪戦になってきた。

text:
$AAOI photonics is an AI data-center throughput test.

The important point is not that fiber is faster than copper in the abstract. It is that GPU clusters only become useful when data can move between servers, switches, and accelerators without turning networking into the bottleneck.

That makes optical transceivers a hidden scaling layer. More GPUs create more east-west traffic, more switch bandwidth, and more demand for components that convert electrical signals into light and back again.

Watch data-center orders, transceiver mix, gross margin, customer concentration, and whether AI cluster builds keep lifting optical networking spend after the first hardware excitement fades.

text:
Copper wire has run the world for over a century. The era that replaces it is starting right now, and it might be the most important infrastructure shift of the decade.

Here is the simple version of what is happening.

Inside an AI data center, thousands of chips have to talk to each other constantly. For a hundred years machines did this the same way, by sending electricity down copper wires. That always worked. Until AI.

 The chips now move so much data so fast that copper physically cannot keep up. Push electricity that hard down a wire and it turns into heat, the signal falls apart, and it can barely cross a single rack before it dies.

So the entire industry is ripping out the copper and replacing it with light. Instead of electricity down a wire, data travels as pulses of light through glass fiber. Light runs cooler, travels farther, and carries vastly more information. 

In a world where power and heat are the limits on how much AI you can build, switching to light is not a preference. It is the only way forward.

What makes this generational rather than just another upgrade is the staircase the whole industry is now climbing. 

It went from 400G to 800G, the workhorse today, already shipping in the tens of millions. 

Now it is climbing to 1.6T, double the bandwidth in the same space, with NVIDIA's newest systems needing over 160 of these per single rack. 

Next comes 3.2T, doubling it again. 

And what used to be a five year upgrade cycle has collapsed to one or two years. These generations are not replacing each other one at a time. 800G, 1.6T, and 3.2T are ramping on top of one another all at once.

Now the size of the prize. The optical transceiver market sat around $15 billion in 2025 and is heading toward $36 billion by 2033, with some forecasts for the AI portion alone reaching far higher. 

The silicon photonics piece inside it grows from under $1 billion to over $6 billion by 2030. This is a market doubling and then doubling again, driven by a buildout that has barely started.

What does it actually mean? It means every company that makes the lasers, the chips, and the modules that turn electricity into light gets paid on every step up that staircase, for years.

$AAOI makes its own lasers in-house, the exact bottleneck part the whole industry is fighting over. 

$CRDO makes the chips that keep these blazing fast connections clean and reliable. 

$LITE and $COHR are the photonics giants supplying the lasers and transceivers everyone depends on. 

$MRVL and $AVGO build the custom silicon at the center of it all.

Copper carried the last hundred years. Light carries the next hundred. And the wiring is being torn out and replaced right now.

text:
In my opinion these are the best positioned companies in their respective niches. 

Sectors covered : AI Infrastructure, Memory, Optical Transceivers, Compound Semi, Specialty Foundry, Power Semi, Laser Systems, Autonomous Defense, Quantum. (NFA)

$CIFR - industrial scale HPC data centers on 15 year hyperscaler leases. $11.4B contracted backlog.
They build and own data centers where AI companies park their supercomputers.

$SK Hynix : dominant HBM3E and HBM4 supplier to NVIDIA, DRAM and NAND flash. Gross margin 68%. Revenue +198% YoY. Forward PE: 6.7x. 
Makes the ultra-fast memory stacked directly on top of AI chips, without it the GPU does absolutely nothing.

$AAOI : vertically integrated optical transceiver manufacturer. Makes its own lasers, chips and optics for hyperscaler data center networking in-house.

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor crystal layers that photonics, RF and power chips are built on. Multi-year supply deals with MACOM and Tower Semiconductor.
The special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of.

$XFAB - specialty analog and mixed-signal foundry for automotive, medical and industrial markets. P/S: 1.69x vs peers at 3–5x.
The factory that makes the unglamorous but completely essential chips running in every car, MRI machine and factory floor.

$AOSL - power semiconductor designer and manufacturer. MOSFETs and power ICs for AI data centers, EVs and consumer electronics. 
The chips that control how electricity flows inside every device you own.

$LPKF - precision laser systems for PCB manufacturing, EV battery cell welding and solar panel processing.
The laser machines that drill, cut and weld the boards and batteries inside almost every electronic device and electric car.

$ONDS - autonomous drone systems, rail automation and defense robotics. $1.47B cash on balance sheet. 32% of float is short.
Robots and drones replacing humans in the most dangerous, repetitive and strategically important jobs.

$INFQ - neutral atom quantum computing, sensing and atomic clocks for national security and AI computation. $443M cash on a $3.25B market cap.
Next generation computers built on individual atoms instead of silicon.

text:
Co ty bredzisz, synku?

Co ma M120 Rak wspólnego z Rosomakiem, oprócz podwozia?

Wieża Raka to w pełni polski projekt, to najlepszy technologicznie moździerz samobieżny na świecie.

Wieża ZSSW-30? Najlepsza wieża bezzałogowa na świecie.

Gdzie produkujecie swoje FlyEye i Warmate? A, czekaj, to polska spółka produkuje je na Ukrainie.

To może polsko-fiński ICEYE? Może polska konstelacja Piast i Mikroglob od Creotech?

Gdzie wasze Vigo Photonics? Może chociaż macie swojego Topaza czy dalej korzystacie z polskich rozwiązań?

Wasz Flaming to trochę lepsze V2 z używanymi silnikami z lat 60. Dlatego "produkujecie" tak śmieszne liczby. Po prostu macie ich tyle ile się uda znaleźć 60letnich silników.

text:
Copper is dead. AI data centers need light — and photonics companies can’t build fast enough.

My deepdive on $AAOI & why this could be one of the best buying opportunities 

https://t.co/AbuYzpolre… https://t.co/WVl6onQCSl

Cpo를 알려준분

1️⃣ Serenity는 누구인가 (Fact)
• X 핸들: @aleabitoreddit
• Bio: “AI/Semi Supply Chain Analyst, former RISC-V FDN and AI research scientist. Now publishing free research on AI chokepoints.”
• 과거 Reddit WSB에서 AXTI 공급망 분석으로 유명해진 후 X로 이동.
• 특징: 초기 아이디어에 강한 반대(백래시)를 받지만, 이후 실적·기관 매수·파트너십으로 검증되는 패턴 반복.
• 최근: X 수익 공유 프로그램에서 2주마다 1억 뷰 이상 기록. 한국 주식(NAV 디스카운트 종목) 언급으로도 주목.

2️⃣ 핵심 투자 철학: Chokepoint Theory (Fact)
• “Big Tech(NVDA, MSFT 등)를 사지 말고, 그들이 반드시 의존할 수밖에 없는 물리적 병목(chokepoint)을 찾아라.”
• 방법: AI 데이터센터 → GPU/HBM/Optics 공급망을 바텀업으로 역설계 → 대체 불가능하거나 공급이 극도로 제한된 지점 식별.
• 주요 기준 (Fact 기반으로 자주 언급):
  - Certain Demand (명확한 메가트렌드 수요)
  - Constrained Supply (높은 진입장벽 + 희소 용량)
  - Low Market Attention (시장에서 아직 저평가/무시)
  - Value Capture potential (가격 결정력·점유율 확대 여지)
  - Catalyst (근시일 내 재평가 촉매)

• 대표 테마: Photonics / Silicon Photonics / CPO, InP substrate & laser, Advanced Packaging, Power semi, Memory bottleneck, mlcc등

3️⃣ 대표 사례와 검증 과정 (Fact / Estimate 구분)
Fact:
• AXTI: InP substrate 공급망 분석으로 WSB에서 밴 당할 정도로 반대 받음 → 이후 Reuters·기관 검증, 주가 대폭 상승.
• SIVE: 본인 최고 conviction 아이디어 중 하나. 스웨덴 미디어·일부에서 meme 취급 → Fidelity·JP Morgan 기관 매수 + JBL·GFS 파트너십 공식 발표로 검증.
• AAOI: $30 부근에서 “경영진 scam” 비판 받음 → 이후 광학 모듈 수요 확대와 함께 큰 상승.
• Memory 섹터 (MU, SK Hynix 등): 3개월 전 99% bearish 분위기 → 실제 대규모 상승.

Estimate:
• 정확한 개인 수익률 수치(일부 기사에서 225x 또는 연 +122% 등 언급)는 본인 공식 확인된 Fact 아님. 시장이 최종 검증한다는 입장 강조.
• 현재 아이디어(XFAB, 한국 NAV 디스카운트 종목 등)도 아직 진행 중으로, 추가 검증 필요.

4️⃣ 최근 활동 (2026년 6월 기준, Fact)
• X 수익 공유 프로그램 성과 공개 (2주마다 1억+ 뷰).
• 한국 주식 관심: Daeduck, DB HiTek 관련 NAV 디스카운트, Hyosung 등 언급.
• XFAB: Optical AI DC + Power semi 노출이 있지만 마케팅이 아쉽다는 코멘트.
• Photonics 강조 지속: AMD의 CW laser 확보, SIVE·AAOI 등 독립 용량 중요성.
• 전체적으로 “초기 반대 → 시장 검증” 패턴을 반복 강조.

━━━━━━━━

5️⃣ AI 인프라 공급망 관점에서 의미 (CPO·Photonics 중심)
• 현재 AI 데이터센터 빌드아웃에서 전력 효율·대역폭이 핵심 병목 → CPO(Co-Packaged Optics), InP 기반 laser·substrate, advanced packaging이 핵심 chokepoint로 부상.
• Serenity가 자주 언급하는 AXTI·SIVE·AAOI·COHR·XFAB 등은 CPO·포토닉스·특화 파운드리 영역과 직접 오버랩.
• “Big Tech 대신 upstream bottleneck” 접근은 단기 모멘텀과 달리, 구조적 공급 제약을 선점하려는 전략.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 Base Case (현재 가시성 기준)
AI capex가 가이던스 수준으로 유지될 경우, 주요 chokepoint 기업들은 실적 성장과 기관 관심 확대에 따라 점진적 재평가 가능. 초기 반대 의견이 잦아들면서 일부 종목의 밸류에이션 정상화 진행.

🐂 Bull Case
AI 수요가 가속되고 여러 chokepoint(Photonics + Power + Memory 등)가 동시에 검증될 경우, 시장 주목이 낮았던 소형·중형 공급망 기업들이 비대칭적 upside를 기록할 수 있음. Serenity가 제시한 아이디어가 지속적으로 시장을 앞서가는 패턴 강화.

🐻 Bear Case
AI capex 둔화 또는 빅테크의 수직통합·신규 공급자 등장으로 용량 제약이 예상보다 빠르게 해소될 경우, 소형 chokepoint 기업들의 재평가가 지연되거나 valuation pressure 발생 가능. 매크로·유동성 악화 시 소형주 변동성 확대 리스크.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

※ 이 글은 Serenity의 공개 포스팅과 관련 기사·트래커 사이트(https://t.co/qKzsAqeFlD)를 Fact 중심으로 정리한 분석입니다. 
투자 매수·매도 추천이 아니며, 특정 종목을 사거나 팔라고 권유하는 내용이 아닙니다. 
모든 투자 판단은 본인 책임 하에 충분히 검토하고 결정하시기 바랍니다. DYODD.

Serenity가 제시한 아이디어가 지속적으로 시장을 앞서가는 패턴 강화. 🐻 Bear Case AI capex 둔화 또는 빅테크의 수직통합·신규 공급자 등장으로 용량 제약이 예상보다 빠르게 해소될 경우, 소형 chokepoint 기업들의 재평가가 지연되거나 valuation pressure 발생 가능. 매크로·유동성 악화 시 소형주 변동성 확대 리스크. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ※ 이 글은 Serenity의 공개 포스팅과 관련 기사·트래커 사이트(https://t.co/qKzsAqeFlD)를 Fact 중심으로 정리한 분석입니다. 투자 매수·매도 추천이 아니며, 특정 종목을 사거나 팔라고 권유하는 내용이 아닙니다. 모든 투자 판단은 본인 책임 하에 충분히 검토하고 결정하시기 바랍니다. Coherent - $COHR 

Makes indium phosphide lasers that transmit data between chips at the speed of light.

Copper can't do this at scale.

Silicon photonics is the only way to connect hundreds of thousands of processors in Nvidia's Vera Rubin Ultra platform.

text:
The novel utility is: **you can now build a passive, single-shot, wide-band optical spectrometer with far fewer detector channels than conventional approaches require, on hardware that already exists.**

Breaking that down to its irreducible parts:

**The core utility:** A geometry (the log spiral) deterministically encodes delay/frequency into angle without any active processing. Once encoded, all the information sits on the chip at once — no scanning, no sweeping, one snapshot. You read it with a detector array placed at the right positions.

**Why that matters:** Detector pixels in certain bands (single-photon / infrared / THz) are scarce and expensive — each one is a cryogenic readout channel, a rare fabrication yield, a finite heat budget. To measure a wide band conventionally, you either (1) buy many pixels, or (2) scan serially with one fast detector. Both are constrained. The log spiral lets you (3) use far fewer pixels and still resolve the whole band, because the geometry did the sorting, not the pixel count.

**The concrete win:** On a 48-pixel SNSPD array (commercial ceiling), the log-spiral front end reaches resolving power R ≈ 31 across a 5-decade band (0.5–2.4 µm). A linear front end reaches R ≈ 10 on the same hardware, or needs ~3× more pixels (which don't exist as a single array) to match. That's 20× cheaper hardware for equal measurement quality.

**The novel part:** Nobody has connected this principle (geometry sorting timing into position) across vision, hearing, cortex dynamics, and engineered photonics before, and nobody has built it as a spectrometer. The idea that *passive geometry can do the computational work of an active optical component* — by itself — is what's new. It's not a tweak of existing dispersive instruments; it's a different category of operation.

**What it unlocks:** Any measurement that needs constant relative precision across a huge range, where detector pixels or data rate is the bottleneck, and where you can tolerate a snapshot (non-repeating signal). Single-photon spectroscopy, broadband mid-IR gas sensing, transient astrophysics, maybe quantum sensing. The enabling move is that it's *passive* — no power, no moving parts, no active tuning — which matters when your detector is already cryogenic and every watt counts.

**The honest boundary:** It doesn't beat all alternatives in all cases. Within a narrow band, or where you want absolute (not relative) precision, or where pixels are cheap (visible light), linear dispersion wins. The spiral is specialized. But for its corner — wide-band, expensive-pixel, single-shot regimes — it's not an optimization, it's the only path to the measurement at all.

That's the utility. Not "better spectrometer." More like: "a spectrometer that works where one didn't before."

text:
Absolutely. Washington can fund the science, but quantum won’t scale as a collection of separate sectors:

Cryogenics. Control electronics. Photonics. Vacuum. Packaging. Foundry capacity.

They’re one physical stack, so scaling requires industrializing all of it.

Follow the bottlenecks all the way down to where value accrues.

text:
According to Straits Times, Singapore is key to GlobalFoundries' quest to AI. 

That's because Temasek sold homegrown Chartered Semiconductor to GF and this Singapore fab has since become the most productive for GF.

Also, GF continues to acquire local firms since. Advanced Micro Foundry (AMF) a Singapore Silicon photonics firm in 2025 and possibly an unnamed local semiconductor firm recently.

Context: in the depths of a semiconductor downturn, the question was: keep pouring billions or sell. And being prudent, Temasek chose the latter. text:
Some of the FinX favorites YTD.

$SOFI down 35% 
$ASTS down 1%
$NOW down 39%
$ZETA down 10%
$HOOD down 9%
$ONDS down 11%

At one point. I owned all 6 of these. I sold 5 of them, for gains, some small some more. Now I’m down to 1. $SOFI 

I still believe that all these companies will do well and their stock will go up eventually. I might even buy back at some point. 

BUT

I’m also not blind to the opportunity in front of me, thats been happening all year.

AI Infrastructure
Semis
Memory
Photonics
To name a few

Yes having conviction is very important, but having an open mind works too. 

$DRAM this etf alone might beat 95% of all individual stocks this year.

text:
$IONQ President Trump just signed a Quantum Executive Order today. This isn't a press release. This isn't a pilot program. This is the U.S. government officially declaring quantum computing a national strategic priority — and $IONQ is sitting at the center of it.

The EO mandates a national goal to build the first quantum computer powerful enough to unlock "quantum-enabled scientific discovery." It directs the Department of Energy, Department of Defense, Commerce, and the Intelligence Community to coordinate a whole-of-government push. It calls for quantum-enabled sensors and networks deployed within the next five years. It creates National Quantum Workforce Development Institutes. And it reconstitutes the National Quantum Initiative Advisory Committee while expanding the Quantum Counterintelligence Protection Team.

Back in May, the Trump administration dropped $2 billion in quantum grants and equity stakes across nine companies. $IONQ wasn't on that list. The stock still jumped 12% on the news. Why? Because investors weren't buying individual winners — they were buying the thesis that quantum is now a national priority. Today's Executive Order takes that thesis and puts it in writing at the highest level of the U.S. government.

So why does this matter specifically for $IONQ?

Three reasons.

First, trapped-ion is the architecture that matters most when accuracy is the requirement. IonQ's technology starts with fewer errors and higher gate fidelity than superconducting approaches. When you're building a system designed for "quantum-enabled scientific discovery," you can't afford errors at scale. Especially with government mass and military operations that are national security dependent. Trapped-ion gives you the accuracy foundation that other architectures are still trying to engineer their way toward.

Second, domestic supply chain is now a mandate — and IonQ already built one. The acquisition of $SKYT will solidify this. The EO explicitly directs the development of adequate domestic supply chain and manufacturing capabilities for quantum technologies. $IONQ already working with $SKYT Technology, a U.S.-based foundry, to keep its quantum supply chain on American soil. They built this before it was required. That's not a coincidence — that's a company that understood where government priorities were heading and positioned accordingly.

Third, the government equity omission from May may be temporary. Prediction markets on Kalshi (Not that it matters) are currently placing odds that IonQ receives a direct U.S. government equity stake in 2026. For context, that's roughly the same odds given to $RGTI — and Rigetti was included in the May round. The door is not closed.

Now let's talk about where $IONQ actually stands fundamentally, because headlines don't pay the bills — execution does.

IonQ reported $130 million in revenue for full-year 2025, making it the first pure-play quantum computing company to cross the $100 million revenue threshold. Management has guided for approximately $270 million in 2026 — nearly doubling year-over-year at the midpoint. Wall Street consensus projects revenue surpassing $1.4 billion by 2030. The company also reported $470 million in remaining performance obligations as of May 2026, which signals strong forward revenue visibility — this isn't speculative pipeline, it's contracted backlog.

On the technology roadmap, IonQ is currently rolling out its fifth-generation Tempo system and has a sixth-generation 256-qubit platform in development. That next-gen system uses electronic controls instead of lasers — simpler architecture, lower cost, more scalable. The acquisition of Oxford Ionics was designed to accelerate exactly this transition. The long-term roadmap targets 10,000 qubits and, eventually, two million physical qubits by 2030. The EO calls for a computer powerful enough to initiate the era of quantum-enabled scientific discovery. IonQ published a roadmap to get there. Most peers haven't.

Northland raised its price target on $IONQ to $70 from $55 today, maintaining an Outperform rating. The firm cited confidence that the company's investor day on September 8 will demonstrate its leadership in achieving "Broad Quantum Advantage."

$IONQ is currently trading around $58–$59. The bulls and the skeptics are essentially at the same number right now, which tells you the market is pricing in execution risk alongside the upside. That's actually a more honest setup than a stock that's already priced for perfection.

I want to be transparent about the bear case too, because credibility matters more than hype.

$IONQ remains EBITDA negative with heavy losses continuing despite the revenue growth. The May $2 billion round excluded them — some analysts have called that a "comparative disadvantage" for companies not receiving the government's implicit endorsement. And the valuation at roughly 73x forward price-to-sales requires a lot of things to go right. None of this is a reason to ignore what happened today. But it is a reason to size positions based on your own risk tolerance rather than headlines.

Today's EO is also not just an $IONQ story.

The entire quantum sector just received a presidential mandate. $QBTS received government investment in the May round. $RGTI is superconducting-based and also in that round. $QUBT is a smaller-cap photonics play with higher volatility. the CEO of $INFQ was there as well. $IBM is building the Anderon foundry with $1 billion in government backing — America's first purpose-built quantum foundry. The EO calls for a whole-of-government approach across DOE, DoD, Commerce, and the Intelligence Community. That's broad enough to benefit every serious player in the space. The rising tide thesis just got a signed Executive Order behind it.

🟥 ⛏️ 🔎 Blue Amber is a rare and captivating mineraloid, not a true mineral, formed from ancient tree resin that fluoresces a deep blue under sunlight.
YT 🎞 Please visit: @olunrockhoplapidary
🔁 ❤️ Greatly Appreciated                                                                 

Found primarily in the Dominican Republic.

It dates back to the Miocene Epoch, roughly 15 to 20 million years ago, preserving botanical inclusions and volatile compounds that reveal the tropical ecosystems of its time.

Unlike mineral crystals, this mineraloid originates from the hardened resin of prehistoric Hymenaea trees, its blue fluorescence caused by aromatic hydrocarbons that interact with sunlight.

When viewed under ultraviolet light, Blue Amber glows with an ethereal blue fire, a natural optical effect that distinguishes it from golden or honey ambers.

Its rarity and organic origin make it a geological and chemical marvel, bridging paleobotany and photonics in one radiant specimen

This phenomenon makes Blue Amber a window into prehistoric ecosystems, preserving botanical chemistry from millions of years ago.

Its luminous glow and organic origin remind us that even fossilized resin can hold the living pulse of Earth’s history.
#BlueAmber

text:
👀some anons tried telling you what it was 10 years ago and they laugh...

“Kraken” — DOE’s monster cryogenic quantum platform
Kraken is the nickname for one of the world’s largest dilution refrigerators, built by Bluefors and installed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL).

“The Seven Dwarfs” — the seven DOE Quantum Testbeds
This is the part most people online misunderstand.
Inside the U.S. Department of Energy’s quantum program, the “Seven Dwarfs” is an informal nickname for the seven major national quantum testbeds distributed across DOE labs.

Snow White & Seven Dwarfs

🧠 The “Seven Dwarfs” — DOE Quantum Testbeds (List Format)
1. Sleepy — Argonne National Laboratory (Q‑NEXT)

2. Grumpy — Sandia National Laboratories (QSCOUT ion‑trap testbed)

3. Doc — Fermilab (SQMS superconducting quantum materials center)

4. Happy — Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (Berkeley Quantum Testbed)

5. Bashful — Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (PNNL quantum sensing + photonics)

6. Sneezy — Los Alamos National Laboratory (LANL quantum simulation + annealing)

7. Dopey — Oak Ridge National Laboratory (QSC + Kraken dilution refrigerator + Frontier integration)

Everyone forgot about Odin...

text:
$SIVE quietly has a consumer sensing business almost nobody is modeling. Their own product page spells it out.

→ DFB lasers for "biometric and health monitoring"

→ Target customers: "OEMs and device manufacturers developing optical sensing modules for wearables, AR/VR systems, health monitors, and smart home devices"

→ Output power up to 100mW across 1200-2400nm wavelengths

→ Ultra-low FIT rates (<1) — the reliability bar consumer electronics demand

→ Optimized for hybrid silicon photonics integration
Why these specs matter:

The 1200-2400nm range is the band used for optical biosensing through skin — the exact wavelengths relevant to non-invasive health monitoring like blood glucose and blood oxygen. The sub-1 FIT rate is the failure tolerance a company shipping tens of millions of consumer units would require.

Now connect it to what the company has said:

→ CEO Vathulya: "two technologies feeding three supercycles"

→ Management has repeatedly flagged sensing and biosensing as optionality the market isn't pricing

Here's the discipline point: Sivers names no customers on this page. None. So anyone telling you exactly who's buying is guessing.

What's NOT a guess: $SIVE has a productized, consumer-grade, health-sensing laser line, built to the reliability spec that mass-market wearables require, sitting inside a company the market is valuing almost entirely on its AI datacenter story.

The wearables/biosensing leg is real. It's just not priced.

$SIVE

text:
Buzzberg Daily Market Radar — today's top Alpha Signals.

$ASTS — AST SpaceMobile is down ~45% in three weeks, and that's exactly where the bulls are stepping in. @photonbull is adding near $74 ("the bottom may be close"), @babyfolio went long at $79, @ckcapitalxx holds it at ~4% of book — a coordinated dip-buy on the satellite-to-cell story. Contested, though: @Temple_Eight is calling for a flush toward $50.
Watch: whether ~$74 holds as the bottom (the bulls' line) vs a break toward $50 (the bear's target)

$CRDO  — Credo is the asymmetric copper→optics bet: AEC dominance if photonics slips, in-house optics if it accelerates — it wins either way. Revenue 6x in two years; Q3 FY26 $407M (+201% YoY) at 49.6% operating margin. . Watch: optics/SiPho mix + whether the ~49% op margin holds as it scales.

$MU — Not a commodity memory name anymore: the Anthropic deal locks multi-year HBM/DRAM/SSD supply and makes MU embedded AI infrastructure. Trades ~10x fwd PE vs oil majors at 13-15x. Earnings Wednesday. 5 bulls. Watch: Wed's print — HBM ASP + guidance.

$PENG — The pure CXL memory-wall bet, still priced like a sleepy box-builder at ~1x sales. The KV-Cache Server is guided to 50%+ of revenue, CXL is +63% YoY, and management doubled guidance.@Temple_Eight is long from $28.50 with a $220-270 bull targe. Watch: whether CXL/KV-Cache actually clears 50% of revenue next print.

$HPSP (403870.KQ) — Korean 소부장 (semi-equipment) names are re-rating off a deep discount to US peers, and HPSP — a high-pressure hydrogen-annealing near-monopoly — just bounced after 4 straight down days. The 815 Money Talk thesis: Korean equipment/materials trade far cheaper than AMAT/LRCX-type US peers, and the rally is finally broadening beyond Samsung/Hynix into 소부장 (PSK +17%, Fadu, Simmtech, HPSP). Watch: HPSP reclaiming its trend after the 4-day pullback (needs confirmation) + the 소부장 re-rating holding as the memory rally broadens.

text:
🤖 US startup starts work on ‘first utility-scale quantum computer’ in Australia

American startup PsiQuantum has begun construction on a facility in Moreton Bay, Australia, where it plans to build and deploy what it calls the world’s first utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computer.

Unlike many quantum computing efforts that remain focused on laboratory-scale systems, PsiQuantum is pursuing a photonics-based approach designed to scale using existing semiconductor manufacturing processes and optical networking technologies.

“Building a quantum computer that solves real world problems is one of the great engineering challenges of our time. For decades, quantum computing has held the promise of transforming what humanity can achieve through computation, and today in Australia we are beginning to turn that promise into reality,” — said Victor Peng, CEO of PsiQuantum.

The company believes fault-tolerant quantum computers could eventually tackle problems beyond the reach of conventional computing systems. Potential applications include drug discovery, materials development, energy research, logistics, manufacturing, agriculture, and financial modeling.

text:
$MRVL just detailed a major leap in silicon photonics for the AI era. 🔥

Conventional photonic modulators are hitting the diffraction limit of light—they can’t scale fast enough for the insane bandwidth AI clusters will demand.

Plasmonics changes the game. By squeezing light below the diffraction limit at a metal-dielectric interface, Marvell’s team is unlocking speeds >1 THz (10x+ faster than today’s best photonic modulators), devices only ~10 microns long (300–500x smaller), and exponentially higher bandwidth density all inside the existing silicon photonics platform.

No need for exotic new materials. Just smarter physics.

This directly powers the optical interconnects for next-gen scale-up and scale-across AI networks, exactly where Marvell has been building leadership (custom silicon + high-speed optics). They’ve already delivered high-speed SiPho devices with plasmonic tech in 2026, with the roadmap pointing to 3.2T+ modules and big wins in power and space efficiency.

This foundational tech compounds over 5–10 years as AI infrastructure scales. Long-term holders know: connectivity is becoming as critical as the accelerators themselves.

text:
$ALMU - THE PUBLIC PROXY FOR PHOTONICS YOU CAN'T BUY IN ANDURIL OR LIGHTMATTER, STILL PRE-REVENUE

Aeluma rose ~6% to $25 as the AI-optics complex snapped back from yesterday's rate flush. Its pitch: manufacturing III-V compound semiconductors — the lasers and detectors photonics needs — directly on 300mm silicon wafers with CMOS-compatible processes, partnered with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo, with quantum-dot lasers aimed at data-center co-packaged optics.

Be honest about the stage: fiscal-2026 revenue is guided to just $4.2–4.6M from R&D contracts, not production hardware, with no named commercial design win yet. This is a call option on an inevitability — the bet that scalable III-V-on-silicon is the missing manufacturing layer when CPO goes volume — not a revenue story today.

text:
$AEHR - THE BURN-IN BOTTLENECK NOBODY PRICES UNTIL AI CHIPS START FAILING IN THE FIELD

Aehr Test Systems rose ~7% to $113 as investors re-rated its wafer-level burn-in franchise into a William Blair presentation and a fresh June deck mapping AI-processor and data-center demand. The company already booked a record $41M production order from a leading hyperscale AI customer this spring and is now chasing a development deal for HBM4E memory-optimized blades, targeting fiscal-2027 orders.

The mechanism is reliability at scale: as SiC, GaN, photonics and HBM go into multi-thousand-GPU racks, every die has to be stressed before it ships — and Aehr is the lone pure-play in wafer-level burn-in across all four, the second derivative of the AI buildout.

text:
$NOK

Nokia is a screaming buy right now

The company has unveiled plans to expand its photonic chip facility in Allentown, Pennsylvania with the additional capacity expected to be operational by the end of Q3 2026

The fact that you can still buy this stock at $13 tells me the market still treats this company as a mobile phone business 

Nokia has taken control of one of the most significant photonics manufacturing facilities in the United States and is now driving a tenfold scale up that is backed by government funding

This stock should easily be $30

text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — June 17, 2026

📊 SMH: $623.97 (+1.29%)

📈 Earnings:
• JBL — $8.8B rev (est $8.66B), $3.16 core EPS. Core operating margin 5.7%, +12% YoY. Raised FY26 outlook to $35B revenue, 5.8% core operating margin, $12.70 core EPS, and $1.4B+ adjusted FCF as AI infrastructure demand stayed extremely strong. Stock finished +1.9%.

🔥 Major Developments:
• AEHR received another follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in system from a major silicon photonics customer serving hyperscale data-center optical interconnects, with additional systems forecast this year as next-gen capacity ramps.
• Intel’s 18A-P process entered risk production, moving its most advanced foundry node closer to external customer qualification and keeping the Apple/advanced-node second-source debate alive.
• NVIDIA Blackwell added day-zero support for MiniMax M3, a 428B-parameter MoE model with 1M-token context, reinforcing the push toward high-throughput agentic inference on Blackwell systems.
• AI infrastructure breadth kept widening: Jabil raised its FY26 outlook on hyperscale demand, while Aehr’s optical burn-in order points to production bottlenecks moving deeper into photonics reliability, test, and manufacturing capacity.

text:
From raw materials to finished systems, here’s how the entire photonics stack is positioned to benefit from exploding demand in AI data centers, optical networking, and high-speed computing.

Layer 1 - Materials & Wafers
 $GLW $AXTI $IQE $AIXA $AMS

Layer 2 - Core Photonic Devices
 $IPGP $COHR $LITE $LASR $SIVE

Layer 3 - Components & Modules
 $AAOI $MTSI $FN $VIAV $LPTH

Layer 4 - Systems & Equipment
 $ASML $BESI $ASM $LPKF $MKS

Layer 5 - Test, Metrology & Yield
 $CAMT $FORM $AEHR $ONTO $VIAV 

Photonics is critical for moving massive amounts of data at the speed of light, exactly what hyperscalers need as AI training scales. 

Many of these names are flying under the radar but sit at the heart of the next leg of AI infrastructure spend.

text:
Very strong results from $JBL in Q3 FY2026

They reported $8.8B in revenue and core EPS of $3.16

Beating market expectations and raising guidance for the fourth quarter

The most relevant part is that they highlighted strong demand in AI infrastructure

Which led them to increase their annual revenue projection for this segment to $13.6B

This is excellent news for the entire supply chain

Especially for companies like $SIVE and $AAOI 

IMO these numbers act as a positive catalyst for $SIVE within a clearly bullish environment for the photonics sector and hyperscalers capex

Very bullish on $SIVE anda $AAOI

text:
I’ve been saying for a while that energy is the next bottleneck after memory/photonics. 

The companies that are able to provide and lend the energy will see MASSIVE returns over the coming years. 

Energy is going to be the most critical component of advancing our infrastructure, whether it is for AI or not. 

Now $NVDA is telling you that energy is more critical than you can imagine. What will you do about it anon?

$SLNH $SMR $ENPH $TE $NUAI

text:
Major dip-buying opportunities in entire market right now:

1. $TE - Second-largest American-owned solar module maker by U.S. capacity, expanding fast, with G2_Austin solar cell production expected in Q4 2026 and 230% YoY revenue growth.

2. $SATL - Space intelligence play with a vertically integrated Earth-observation constellation . Q1 revenue grew 80% YoY, with sovereign defense contracts and Merlin AI defense constellation as key catalysts.

3. $ONDS - Autonomous defense platform across drones, counter-UAS, ISR, and ground systems. Q1 revenue grew 1,065% YoY, $PLTR partnership, and defense acquisitions.

4. $MRAM -MRAM memory leader supplying persistent memory for industrial, defense, data center, and transportation use cases. Q1 revenue grew 13% YoY, with a new $40M U.S. defense contract and Microchip foundry agreement 

5. $LWLG - AI photonics materials play using proprietary electro-optic polymers to move data faster with lower power.

6. $IREN - AI cloud/data-center infrastructure play. Major catalyst is its $NVDA 5GW strategic partnership and multibillion-dollar AI cloud expansion. Major rerate coming soon. 

7. $IONQ - Quantum computing leader building trapped-ion systems, quantum networking, sensing, and security platforms. and President Trump recently signed new executive orders focused on quantum computing.

8. $HYLN - Distributed power play with fuel-agnostic KARNO generators for data centers, defense, and mobile power. Q1 revenue rose from $0.5M to $2.8M, with Navy KARNO work, data-center LOIs, and military contract pipeline as catalysts.

9. $FLY - Space and defense company across launch, lunar landers, and orbital vehicles. Q1 revenue grew about 45% YoY.

10. $EOSE - American-made zinc battery storage play focused on long-duration energy storage. Q1 revenue grew 445% YoY.

11. $AXTI - Semiconductor materials play supplying InP/GaAs substrates used in AI data-center optical connectivity. Q1 revenue grew about 39% YoY, with indium phosphide capacity expansion.

12. $AMPX - Silicon-anode battery leader targeting drones, defense, aerospace, and electric mobility. Q1 revenue grew 153% YoY, with U.S. military demand.

13. $AAOI - AI optical transceiver play ramping 800G and 1.6T products for hyperscale data centers. Q1 revenue grew about 51% YoY, with 800G volume shipments and capacity expansion in the U.S.

14. $RKLB - End-to-end space company combining launch, spacecraft, components, and defense programs. Q1 revenue grew 63.5% YoY, with record backlog, Neutron/Electron contracts.

15. $ASTS - Direct-to-cell satellite broadband play aiming to connect normal phones from space. Q1 revenue grew from $718K to $14.7M, with satellite deployment.

16. $VOYG -  Defense and space technology platform tied to strategic systems, propulsion, Starlab, and national security programs. Recent Golden Dome award winners.

text:
The market is rotating OUT of hardware/capex-heavy names and INTO defensives + consumer staples. This is a classic risk-off signal within an otherwise mixed tape.

Watch carefully — when Tech Equipment sells off this hard, it often precedes either:
A buying opportunity in semis & photonics (our lane)
Or a broader de-risking cycle

We stay patient. Base-builders don’t panic on one red day.

Not financial advice.

text:
🚨 SCIENTISTS JUST USED A 200-YEAR-OLD LIGHT TRICK TO CREATE OPTICAL SKYRMIONS NO EXPENSIVE METAMATERIALS REQUIRED.

Researchers at NTU Singapore have generated stable optical skyrmions using the classic Poisson spot a bright point that appears in the center of a circular object’s shadow when illuminated by coherent laser light. This simple diffraction effect, first debated in the 19th century, now produces complex topological patterns in light.

In a single light spot, the team created four different types of optical skyrmions at once: spin skyrmions, Stokes skyrmions, electric-field skyrmions, and magnetic-field skyrmions. These swirling, hedgehog-like structures in light’s properties are normally created with complex engineered metamaterials.

Why this matters:

• Optical skyrmions are stable, particle-like patterns in light that could one day be used for high-density data storage, optical communications, and topological computing
• Previous methods required expensive, precisely fabricated metamaterials
• This new approach uses only a laser and a small circular disk dramatically simpler and more accessible
• One setup generates multiple skyrmion types simultaneously, allowing researchers to study how they interact

The deeper implication:

Skyrmions were originally a concept from particle physics. They later appeared in magnetic materials and are now being explored in photonics. 

By making optical skyrmions much easier to create and control, this work lowers the barrier for studying topological light and could accelerate applications in future information technologies.

We’re moving from “we can make these exotic light structures with heroic effort” to “we can make them with something almost anyone with a laser can try.”

How do you think topological structures in light could change optical computing or data storage in the coming decades?

Follow for more frontier photonics and topological physics breakthroughs.

Researchers at NTU Singapore have generated stable optical skyrmions using the classic Poisson spot a bright point that appears in the center of a circular object’s shadow when illuminated by coherent laser light. This simple diffraction effect, first debated in the 19th century, now produces complex topological patterns in light. In a single light spot, the team created four different types of optical skyrmions at once: spin skyrmions, Stokes skyrmions, electric-field skyrmions, and magnetic-field skyrmions. These swirling, hedgehog-like structures in light’s properties are normally created with complex engineered metamaterials. Why this matters: • Optical skyrmions are stable, particle-like patterns in light that could one day be used for high-density data storage, optical communications, and topological computing • Previous methods required expensive, precisely fabricated metamaterials • This new approach uses only a laser and a small circular disk dramatically simpler and more accessible • One setup generates multiple skyrmion types simultaneously, allowing researchers to study how they interact The deeper implication: Skyrmions were originally a concept from particle physics. They later appeared in magnetic materials and are now being explored in photonics. By making optical skyrmions much easier to create and control, this work lowers the barrier for studying topological light and could accelerate applications in future information technologies. We’re moving from “we can make these exotic light structures with heroic effort” to “we can make them with something almost anyone with a laser can try.” How do you think topological structures in light could change optical computing or data storage in the coming decades? text:
InGaAS 1060nm VCSEL:

- About 1 dB insertion loss
- 8x100G PAM4 over 2 km (2km !!)

Take note Photonics frens bc if the VCSEL Laser and µLEDs work, CPO landscape will be different.... 

$LITE $MRVL $AVGO
embedded_text:
people usually use multimode fiber (MMF, OM3 and OM4 etc) with ~850 nm VCSELs. At 1060 nm you need custom MMF, or a ~1 um wavelength single mode fiber (SMF-28 for 1.5 um is multimode here).

guess what the insertion loss is between the 1060 nm VCSEL and the SMF? https://t.co/t8kA8HMgl5

text:
$SIVE / $SIVEF DD Thread 🧵 all credit to: @aleabitoreddit

Sivers Semiconductors is my highest-conviction laser chokepoint long in the entire photonics/AI supply chain. 

They’re not just another optics name, they’re embedded across multiple next-gen architectures that are about to ramp hard in 2026-2027.

$SIVE + Aeva likely coded from the new PR today and SIVE + Apple. Rumors / speculation.

Core positioning: Reference laser supplier for $GFS (GlobalFoundries) on CPO/NPO programs.

Primary/sole-source or heavily designed-in with Ayar Labs in the $NVDA NVLink CPO ecosystem.
Working with $JBL (and other pluggable players) on 1.6T LRO pluggable transceivers using CW lasers. This design-around of EML bottlenecks created a “relatively dramatic moat” per Jabil management. 

After the JBL PR, other pluggable makers reached out—active co-development/qualification happening now (not public yet).

Laser supplier for $POET (scale-out CPO H2 2026+), and deeply mapped into $MRVL Celestial, Lightmatter, Lightelligence, and others for scale-up CPO (main volume ramp H2 2027).

O-Net building ELS (external light sources) with Sivers lasers for Asian hyperscaler supply chains.
Not limited to scale-up CPO—pluggables, scale-out CPO, NPO, etc. Immediate revenue path in 1.6T pluggables starting soon.

AMD angle: Trendforce reported AMD is scrambling for CW laser supply to avoid being constrained by $NVDA. $SIVE is perfectly positioned via GFS reference design + direct hyperscaler relationships. $AAOI is the other obvious Western name here.

Consumer & robotics upside (cherry on top):Apple relationship likely ongoing (website still lists “Optical sensing modules for wearables” for biometric/health monitoring). Recent PRs appear to be defensive against short-seller claims the relationship ended—Sivers has probably been working with Apple for a while.
Aeva + robotics/physical AI path: Sivers lasers powering Boston Dynamics Atlas (via LG Innotek) and Nvidia Hyperion ecosystem. Possible Aeva integration.

Defense/Space catalyst: New $8.2M production order for Ka-Band Beamforming ICs with https://t.co/0oKZCt6NZc (post their acquisition by larger defense prime). This opens follow-on volume contracts in the Space/Defense supply chain—another chokepoint layer on top of photonics. Nasdaq listing & growth path:General meeting expected to confirm Nasdaq listing timeline soon. This unlocks liquidity + M&A firepower to vertically integrate downstream (make each laser more valuable) exactly like $LITE did.

$LITE went from ~$2.88B MC in 2024 to ~$67B MC in 2026. $SIVE is at a similar early-stage valuation today with far broader design-ins across the entire optical stack.

Institutional validation (huge signal): JP Morgan disclosed buying ~5.25% of the company in one month (from tiny stake to major position). $135M is spare change to US institutions—they can easily take 25%+ if float is available.

BlackRock entered as passive holder post-index inclusion.

Fidelity Research showing active positions.

This is classic retail-frontrun → institutional accumulation pattern we’ve seen before ( $NBIS, $RKLB, etc.). Float was heavily shorted by Swedish funds; big US money buying is a massive cover signal.

Why this is different from other optics names:

Markets still treat $SIVE like a pure future-CPO story, but the real edge is multi-architecture coverage + immediate pluggable ramp + ability to do M&A post-Nasdaq. They’re everywhere hyperscalers are building next-gen optical interconnects. Revenue visibility is still low because most relationships are confidential, but the supply-chain mapping is extremely strong.

Short-term volatility is expected (high-beta semis got crushed recently), but the setup into 2027 volume ramps looks exceptional. This is why it’s my favorite laser chokepoint long—qualified into the right places at the right time. Not financial advice. DYODD. This is a synthesis of public posts from @aleabitoreddit

text:
After too many hours of research/DD to count, these are my Top 5 AI Stocks in the Top 5 AI Sectors to Invest in:

1. Neoclouds: The GPU & Compute landlords. Whoever owns the compute owns what every AI company on the earth needs to grow & succeed.

$NBIS - Nebius. Spun out of Yandex, rebuilt as a pure-play AI cloud. Microsoft and Meta committed billions in contracted backlog. The fastest growing neocloud on the board. My favorite pick as a long-term investment.

$CRWV - CoreWeave. The American-founded neocloud. SemiAnalysis rated this Neocloud in a class of it's own, which can not be understated.  Deals with Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, Perplexity, Google, Microsoft... the list goes on forever. 

$HUT -  Hut 8. Started as a bitcoin miner, pivoted hard into AI hosting and power infrastructure. Owns the power, not just the GPUs. They're landing deals left and right, definitely an underappreciated sleeper pick.

$APLD - Applied Digital. Same playbook. Former crypto miner now building purpose-designed AI data center campuses with long-term hyperscaler leases.

$CIFR - data center host for Anthropic's directly, purchased Google TPU v7 Ironwoods (400K units, ~$10B), backed by a 10-year, $3B+ Fluidstack hosting deal where Google guarantees $1.4B of lease obligations for a 5.4% equity stake. One of the only neocloud-adjacent names with real exposure to Google's silicon instead of pure Nvidia GPU rental.

2. Optical / Photonics:

Because every GPU is useless if it can't talk to the GPU next to it.

$CRDO - Credo. The connectivity layer inside every AI cluster. Active electrical cables and DSPs that scale with every rack hyperscalers deploy.

$LITE - Lumentum. Legacy telecom optics company that's become a core 800G/1.6T transceiver supplier for the AI buildout.

$ALAB - Astera Labs. Connectivity chips that solve the bottleneck between GPUs, memory, and storage. Pure-play AI infrastructure with almost no legacy drag.

$COHR - Coherent. Lasers, optical components, and transceivers spanning the entire photonics stack from datacom to industrial.

$MRVL - Marvell. Custom silicon and photonic fabric for hyperscalers. The company quietly inside more AI racks than people realize.

3. Memory:

The most cyclical, most violently mispriced sector in semis. HBM demand changed the entire setup.

$DRAM - DRAM ETF. One ticker, every important memory company on the planet, including South Korean companies like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronic, companies you CAN'T INVEST IN with most brokerages.

$SNDK - SanDisk. Spun off from Western Digital, now a pure-play NAND flash story riding the same supply tightness as everyone else in this sector.

$MU - Micron. One of three companies on Earth that makes HBM. The clearest direct line from AI buildout to memory revenue.

$WDC - Western Digital. Hard drive and enterprise storage demand riding the same data center capex wave as everything else on this list.

$STX - Seagate. The other half of the storage duopoly. Enterprise nearline drives are seeing the same supply crunch dynamics as memory.

4. Analog / Power Semis:

Unsexy. Necessary. Every data center, every EV, every robot needs power management silicon that doesn't get the AI premium yet:

$MXL - MaxLinear. Smaller-cap analog and mixed-signal play with infrastructure and data center exposure that's still flying under the radar.

$STM - STMicroelectronics. European chip giant spanning auto, industrial, and power semis. Way out of favor relative to its diversification.

$ON - ON Semiconductor. Power semis for EVs and industrial, now leaning harder into data center power delivery as a growth vector.

$VSH - Vishay. Passive components: resistors, capacitors, diodes. Boring until you realize literally everything electronic needs them.

$POWI - Power Integrations. High-voltage power conversion chips. Small cap, niche, and positioned for the power efficiency problem AI data centers haven't solved yet.

5. Physical AI / Robotics:

It's coming, soon, and the market is beginning to realize it.

$OUST - Ouster. Lidar for robotics, industrial, and autonomy. Consolidated the space after merging with Velodyne, now the survivor.

$VICR - Vicor. High-density power modules for robotics, AI servers, and defense. Power delivery at the component level, not the rack level.

$VPG - Vishay Precision Group. Precision sensors and strain gauges. The torque and force-sensing hardware that gives robots a sense of touch.

$AEVA - Aeva. 4D lidar with built-in velocity sensing. Smaller and earlier stage than the rest of this list, highest risk, highest ceiling.

$AMBA - Ambarella. Edge AI vision chips. Powers the cameras and perception systems inside cars, robots, and security infrastructure.

I believe a portfolio with these 25 names will severely outperform the market over the next few years. I have 7 figures throughout many of these names. These are all names I'm currently invested in, or plan on investing in in the near future.

None of this is financial advice.

Active electrical cables and DSPs that scale with every rack hyperscalers deploy. $LITE - Lumentum. Legacy telecom optics company that's become a core 800G/1.6T transceiver supplier for the AI buildout. $ALAB - Astera Labs. Connectivity chips that solve the bottleneck between GPUs, memory, and storage. Pure-play AI infrastructure with almost no legacy drag. $COHR - Coherent. Lasers, optical components, and transceivers spanning the entire photonics stack from datacom to industrial. $MRVL - Marvell. Custom silicon and photonic fabric for hyperscalers. The company quietly inside more AI racks than people realize. 3. Memory: The most cyclical, most violently mispriced sector in semis. HBM demand changed the entire setup. $DRAM - DRAM ETF. One ticker, every important memory company on the planet, including South Korean companies like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronic, companies you CAN'T INVEST IN with most brokerages. $SNDK - SanDisk. Spun off from Western Digital, now a pure-play NAND flash story riding the same supply tightness as everyone else in this sector. $MU - Micron. One of three companies on Earth that makes HBM. The clearest direct line from AI buildout to memory revenue. $WDC - Western Digital. Hard drive and enterprise storage demand riding the same data center capex wave as everything else on this list. $STX - Seagate. The other half of the storage duopoly. Enterprise nearline drives are seeing the same supply crunch dynamics as memory. 4. Analog / Power Semis: Unsexy. Necessary. Every data center, every EV, every robot needs power management silicon that doesn't get the AI premium yet: $MXL - MaxLinear. Smaller-cap analog and mixed-signal play with infrastructure and data center exposure that's still flying under the radar. $STM - STMicroelectronics. European chip giant spanning auto, industrial, and power semis. Way out of favor relative to its diversification. $ON - ON Semiconductor. Power semis for EVs and industrial, now leaning harder into data center power delivery as a growth vector. $VSH - Vishay. Passive components: resistors, capacitors, diodes. Boring until you realize literally everything electronic needs them. $POWI - Power Integrations. High-voltage power conversion chips. Small cap, niche, and positioned for the power efficiency problem AI data centers haven't solved yet. 5. Physical AI / Robotics: It's coming, soon, and the market is beginning to realize it. $OUST - Ouster. Lidar for robotics, industrial, and autonomy. Consolidated the space after merging with Velodyne, now the survivor. $VICR - Vicor. High-density power modules for robotics, AI servers, and defense. Power delivery at the component level, not the rack level. $VPG - Vishay Precision Group. Precision sensors and strain gauges. The torque and force-sensing hardware that gives robots a sense of touch. $AEVA - Aeva. 4D lidar with built-in velocity sensing. Smaller and earlier stage than the rest of this list, highest risk, highest ceiling. $AMBA - Ambarella. Edge AI vision chips. Powers the cameras and perception systems inside cars, robots, and security infrastructure. I believe a portfolio with these 25 names will severely outperform the market over the next few years. I have 7 figures throughout many of these names. These are all names I'm currently invested in, or plan on investing in in the near future. text:
Everyone talks about AI chips.

Almost nobody talks about what actually limits them.

Imagine a Formula 1 car.

Now imagine fueling it through a drinking straw.

That’s modern AI.

The problem isn’t computing power anymore.

It’s moving data.

For decades, the semiconductor industry got faster by making transistors smaller.

But physics eventually showed up.

You can’t keep building bigger chips forever:
• Larger chips are harder to manufacture.
• More defects = lower yields.
• Costs explode.

So the industry found a workaround:

➡️ Build many small chips.
➡️ Place them extremely close together.
➡️ Connect them so tightly they behave like one giant chip.

This is called Advanced Packaging.

And it’s becoming just as important as the chip itself.

Then came HBM (High Bandwidth Memory):

➡️ Memory is placed right next to the AI processor.
➡️ Data travels millimeters instead of centimeters.
➡️ Massive speed gains.
➡️ Lower power consumption.

Without HBM and advanced packaging, modern AI wouldn’t exist.

The next step?

Photonics.

Instead of moving data with electrons, move it with light.

Less power.
More bandwidth.
Longer distances.

The AI infrastructure stack is becoming:

Transistors → Packaging → HBM → Photonics

Potential winners across the stack:

• NVIDIA — AI compute
• AMD — AI compute
• TSMC — advanced packaging leader
• BESI — advanced packaging equipment
• ASMPT — packaging equipment
• Amkor — packaging services
• SK Hynix — HBM leader
• Micron — HBM
• Samsung — HBM
• Ayar Labs — optical interconnects
• Broadcom — networking & optics
• Marvell — optical networking
• Sivers Semiconductors — lasers & photonics
• Coherent — optical components
• Lumentum — optical components

The market spent 30 years optimizing computation.

The next 30 years may be about optimizing the movement of data.

From first principles: The whole semiconductor industry runs on one engine: Moore's Law. Every couple of years, you could shrink transistors, pack 2x as many onto a chip...and get more compute for the same cost. But the cost of moving a bit of data from one place to another doesn't improve at the same rate as the computation gains. Mainly because moving electrons vast distances costs time/energy. And going off-chip and into another package is super expensive. And ofc you can't just build one giant chips because yields fall exponentially w/ area. Even if you 2x chip size, you can lose most of the yield. This is where you stop thinking about the chip itself, but more about the package. Advanced packaging. Very simply: If you can't make one big chip, make loads of smaller ones + connect them so tightly that they act like one big chip. You get the benefits of a giant chip without paying the yield penalty. By decreasing power consumption + increasing speed. And today, bandwidth is probably the defining problem for AI accelerators especially. Advanced packaging provides that bandwidth by putting HBM next to the logic + wiring it together with a higher volume of shorter/denser connections. You literally can't route enough wires across a circuit board to feed a modern GPU cos the bandwidth those chips need only exists if the memory is packaged right up against the compute. There's ofc more depth to this like specific advanced packaging components or technologies. Including HBM's role in the whole thing and stuff like glass substrates too. But that's a short story if anyone wanted to learn a bit more rather than getting a bunch of random ticker symbols. But fwiw, my favourite pure-play advanced packaging equipment name is $BESI that I hold. If anyone was curious and wanted to do more research."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2067708489925800396"}}, "rest_id": "2067964372530434146", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "110373", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2068065579404030328", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "516", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Everyone thinks quantum computing is about advanced theoretical physics. 

I analyzed 350+ quantum job listings and the reality is much different.

It turns out you are basically building fridges and writing control software.

Combined Skill Demand (% of listings asking for it):

1/ Dominant:
- Python ~50% 
- Qiskit ~50% 
- Quantum Control Systems ~50%
- Cryogenics ~45% 
- FPGA Programming ~35% 
- Quantum Error Correction ~35% 
- Fault-Tolerant Computing ~35% 

2/ Common:
- Superconducting Qubits ~25% 
- PyTorch ~20% 
- Microwave Engineering ~15% 
- Logical Qubits ~15% 
- Dilution Refrigerators ~15% 
- Photonics ~12% 

3/ Niche 
- Post-Quantum Cryptography ~10% 
- Surface Codes ~8% 
- Photonic Qubits ~8% 
 - Trapped Ions ~7% 
- Process Integration / SPC ~6% 
- Semiconductor ~5% 
- Quantum Phase Estimation ~5% 
- OpenQASM ~5% 
- PennyLane ~3% 

Bottom line: 3 things most quantum roles want

1. Python + Qiskit (~50%)
2. Quantum Control Systems (~50%)
3. Cryogenics (~45%)

text:
Q1 2026 was a bumper quarter in venture capital. Investors deployed $300 billion of fresh capital, more than double the previous quarter and surpassing 70% of all venture spending in 2025.

The devil is, of course, in the detail: $188 billion of that cash went to four companies – OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo – accounting for 60% of all venture capital deployed in Q1.

VCs generally revel in change, but even by our standards what it takes to raise has shifted at an unprecedented rate over the past 12 months: expectations of early-stage founders – in terms of defensibility, scaling, and pricing – have been completely rewritten.

Fearing the ‘AI-pocalypse’, early-stage founders could be forgiven for feeling despondent seeing these figures. The outlook looks daunting, but it doesn’t need to be. The concentration of capital around a handful of AI giants is changing the rules, but the game goes on. The question for founders right now is not whether Big AI wins, but how can smaller companies survive and thrive alongside it.

A Rising Tide Is Lifting Most Boats

Investment in the US rose 190% YoY in Q1 but deal count fell 26%, meaning that capital is concentrating into fewer, larger cheques. However, the remaining $112 billion of funding sits in line with recent quarterly highs, and the environment for smaller rounds remains robust.

Frontier lab mega-rounds do not directly compete with early-stage cheques: indeed, pre-Seed to Series A companies are growing faster than ever before. Stripe recently revealed data showing that the top 100 AI-native companies are scaling from $1 million to $30 million ARR five times faster than previous software generations.

It’s still a very good time to be an early-stage AI company, especially one that can move quickly and provide genuine agentic solutions rather than simple GPT wrappers. That distinction matters because the startups most under threat are those with no meaningful moat – i.e. businesses that lack sufficient brand, switching costs or economies of scale. These companies will be squeezed not only by platform owners from above them, but also by falling prices below them.

Learnings from the SaaSpocalypse

It wasn’t so long ago that Anthropic’s Claude Cowork launched and within hours wiped hundreds of millions off the valuations of some of the world’s leading software companies. Investors and founders alike feared that a new army of AI agents was about to eat everybody’s lunch.

As of June 2026, however, debate on the death of SaaS is ongoing. Enterprise customers could theoretically vibe code much of their own software, but very few are actually going to do so. At least not yet. At the same time, the opposing view – that concerns about model companies are entirely overblown – has become equally unhelpful. The phrase “what if model companies do this?” has become a catch-all objection that often substitutes for harder conversations around business fundamentals.

The right question is not whether model companies will kill software. They won’t. It’s which software companies will become stronger as models improve, and which will become increasingly commoditised by them. That distinction matters because the bar to raise has doubled while investor attention has narrowed dramatically. Raising money outside AI is a tough ask in 2026, but that doesn’t mean every company needs to become an AI company. It means founders need to ask harder questions.

If OpenAI or Anthropic launched this feature tomorrow, what would still belong to us? Are we building a product, or are we building an advantage? Do we control proprietary data, hardware, scientific know-how, distribution, or regulation? Does our business become stronger as models improve, or weaker? Would customers still choose us if intelligence itself became abundant and cheap? These are the questions investors are asking too.

The Best Offense Is a Good Defense

Whatever you do as an early-stage founder right now, defense is key.

Investors are gravitating towards sectors like robotics, defence, photonics, biotech, and novel compute. This isn’t because they are fashionable, but because they possess characteristics that are difficult for foundation model providers to replicate.

Robotics and defence require integration with the physical world, complex systems, and long customer relationships. Photonics and novel compute rely on years of scientific research and manufacturing expertise. Biotech combines proprietary data, regulatory barriers, and deep domain knowledge. Intelligence alone is not enough. 

Investors are looking for companies with assets that cannot simply be replaced by agents. Proprietary data, specialised hardware, scientific expertise and difficult-to-reproduce infrastructure are becoming durable moats. 

No matter what, we are in for a turbulent remainder of 2026. Capital is concentrating at every level, the revenue bar to raise has roughly doubled in five years, and the companies getting funded look nothing like they did three years ago.

This looks bad for early-stage founders, but it doesn’t need to be. Every technological wave creates new giants, but it also creates new opportunities for smaller players that understand where defensibility lies. For founders, the message is simple but not easy: assume intelligence becomes abundant and build the things that remain scarce.

The opinions expressed in https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs commentary pieces are solely the views of their authors and do not necessarily reflect the opinions and beliefs of Fortune.

text:
Some thoughts on $AXTI…

As I work through the China AI optics supply ramp, the AXT setup keeps getting more complicated.

China is reportedly preparing a ~$295B five-year AI data-center buildout plan.

At the same time, the domestic optics stack is ramping everywhere:

> Source Photonics / Dongshan is expanding optical chips and modules.

> Chinese suppliers are pushing EMLs, CW lasers, InP PICs, silicon photonics and optical engines.

> Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and AXT / Tongmei are expanding InP substrate output.

> Innolight and Eoptolink continue scaling data-center transceivers.

So on one hand, this is clearly positive for AXT’s China-based demand. Domestic China demand is cleaner because the export-permit issue really shows up when substrates leave China.

If China’s AI buildout absorbs more domestic optical supply, AXT could be sitting in the middle of a very important local bottleneck.

But there is another side.

More domestic InP substrate capacity from Yunnan Germanium, Guangdong Xiandao and others could reduce AXT’s pricing power over time if those suppliers scale and qualify.

And the geopolitical angle cuts both ways.

China has real leverage here. If it wanted to slow U.S. substrate availability, it does not need to fully ban exports. It can continue to slow permits, condition approvals, request more end-user data, or prioritize domestic demand.

That would be supportive for China’s own AI optics ecosystem, but it would also make AXT look less like a globally fungible supplier and more like a China-centered strategic substrate asset. That deserves a much different multiple. 

At the same time, China has limits.

They still want Innolight, Eoptolink, Source and others selling into global hyperscalers.

They also still need parts of the global tool stack, especially for high-end InP photonics. China is localizing MOCVD and test where it can, but the most proven high-end InP tool ecosystem still appears heavily tied to companies like $aixa and $veco

So the AXT debate is really evolving. 

InP demand up
China AI buildout up
Domestic substrate competition up
Export-permit risk up
Geopolitical chokepoint value up

That combination can be very bullish, but it also changes the type of business the market is underwriting.

Anyway, just thinking out loud here as I work through this report and come to stronger conclusions. Treat this as a brain dump rather than what my final assumption will be .

Open to dialogue!

text:
Round 2 of high sentiment AI trades with retard explanations at the end. 

In my opinion these are the greatest companies in their respective sectors, I genuienly don't think you could find better. Sectors covered - Cloud servicing, Memory, CPO, NPO, Copper, Testing, Transmitters, Switches (NFA)

$NBIS - pure play AI cloud infrastructure, renting out GPU compute at scale, backed by NVDA.
(you rent their computers to run AI instead of buying your own)

$SNDK (or any other memory play) - NAND flash and SSD maker, riding the AI storage demand wave.
(the chips that store all the data the AI models need to remember)

$MRVL - custom AI silicon and optical/electrical interconnect for hyperscaler data centers.
(the chips that move data between the AI chips, and the custom brains the big clouds order)

$AEHR - test and burnin systems for AI chips and silicon photonics, screening parts before they ship.
(the machines that stress-test chips so the broken ones get caught before they go in a server)

$AAOI - vertically integrated optical transceivers, makes the lasers and optics for data center networking in-house.
(the parts that turn data into light to fire it across the data center)

$IQE - epitaxial wafer supplier, grows the compound semiconductor layers that photonics and RF chips are built on.
(the raw special wafers everyone else builds their laser and signal chips on top of)

$SMTC - high speed signal integrity platform for 800G, 1.6T and 3.2T transmitters.
(the chips that move faster data inside AI clusters)

$CRDO - active electrical cables and SerDes connectivity, making copper carry AI data faster over short reaches.
(the smart cables that let cheap copper move data fast enough for AI racks)
