text:
Why did I even get interested in $IQE?

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Now you understand why $IQE?

When the entire market went absolutely crazy over $AXTI - I was swallowing the bitter pill of being late to that specific party.

But since I hate losing, I immediately started asking myself: what is next?

I always try to look one step ahead. 

That is exactly how I managed to get early into names like $IQE or $LPKF and others before the masses caught on.

But back to $IQE - why this specific company?

The global frenzy and massive demand for optical lasers are just getting started.

The market quickly realized that Indium Phosphide (InP) - the core substrate material - does not just fall from the sky. 

Hence, the massive explosive run on $AXTI.

But a raw InP wafer by itself is practically useless for high-performance photonics.

You have to actually do something with it before it can become a functioning laser.

Epitaxy.

Epitaxy is the atomic-scale deposition of crystal layers onto that substrate.

Only a handful of companies globally possess the highly specialized MOCVD and MBE machinery required for this advanced process.

One company listed on the London Stock Exchange  owns a significantly larger fleet of these reactors than almost anyone else, giving them a massive structural scale advantage.

Say hello to $IQE.

Look at the pure-play competition for epi-wafers. Landmark Optoelectronics possesses far fewer machines, yet because it sits on a different exchange (Taiwan), it historically traded at multiples that make IQE look like an absolute deep-value steal.

And if you want proof of their technological moat: 

IQE was just confirmed as the second largest patent filer in the entire UK for semiconductor devices. 

This isn't a speculative small-cap; it’s an IP powerhouse.

Now, to be completely fair, IQE had a dark cloud hanging over it.

The cyclical semiconductor downturn cut their 2025 revenue to £97m, and because chip fabs have massive fixed costs, underutilization completely crushed their near-term margins.

Worse, geopolitical tensions flared as China introduced strict export controls on critical raw materials like Gallium and Antimony.

If you check most standard financial screeners today, that ugly debt and supply-chain risk warning still pop up.

But the market is looking backward.

First, IQE completely neutralized the China risk by successfully recycling 100% of their Gallium Arsenide and Indium Phosphide wafer waste directly back into ultra-pure raw materials for their production lines.

Second, on May 28, 2026 - the ultimate balance sheet reset happened.

IQE officially completed a transformational £81 million strategic recapitalization package.

MACOM Technology Solutions ($MTSI) stepped up to anchor the deal - injecting £30m in direct equity and £15m in new convertible notes to secure a ~15% strategic stake. 

Existing institutional notes were swapped into equity, and the old HSBC bank debt was entirely wiped out.

The balance sheet is clean. The financial risk is gone.

So, who is MACOM and why does this partnership matter?

MACOM is a heavyweight in high-performance semiconductor products for Data Centers, Aerospace, and Defense. 

They design the brains - the optical components and ICs that power next-generation networks.

By deeply aligning with IQE, MACOM secures a stable Western supply chain for advanced epi-wafers. 

For IQE, it guarantees immediate structural volume demand to fill those empty reactors and unlock massive operating leverage.

And the momentum shift is already visible.

Photonics has officially overtaken wireless to become IQE’s largest segment (58.7% of revenue), propelled by surging AI data center demand and immediate US military and defense funding releases.

Think that is all? 

We got another massive confirmation of their turnaround strategy.

IQE followed this up by solidifying their multi-year agreement with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM).

Together, they are scaling Silicon Photonics (SiPh) via the heterogeneous integration of IQE’s advanced epitaxy layers directly onto Tower’s silicon wafers. 

This allows them to build optical transceivers that handle the insane data transfer speeds required by modern AI architectures.

The pieces of the puzzle are coming together perfectly.

The market is still pricing $IQE like a distressed, underutilized UK small-cap because backward-looking financial data feeds haven't adjusted to the new post-deal reality.

They are missing the bigger picture.

The debt is gone, the largest players in the chip space are anchoring equity deals to secure capacity, and IQE sits right at the choke point of the entire optical computing revolution.

While the crowd is still chasing raw substrates, the smart money is moving to the epitaxy bottleneck.

Now you understand why $IQE?"}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2013188852278517760/rPZTZ1cG_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Mon Dec 29 12:28:10 +0000 2025", "name": "Finn Stockinger", "screen_name": "FinnStockinger"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": false}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjoyMDA1NjE2ODEyNTYyNzg4MzUy", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": true, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Seeking asymmetric edges in quality growth & tech. Sometimes I feel like we get too caught up chasing shiny new objects and completely forget about the old ones.

It's the exact same story with $TRT. 

Massive hype exactly a month ago, only to be forgotten by the masses as it steadily slid lower.

When everyone else forgot about $TRT, I just doubled my position.

Today, history is repeating itself, and I'm just sitting back with a smile - holding my $TRT shares.

Still remember a company called $IQE? 

You better remind yourselves.

I started buying at 12p, then 19p, and added a while ago at 37p. My average is 29p. 

Still holding, the party will come. Sooner or later.

It’s a completely different company today than when I first bought in:

Paid off debt after the $MTSI investment + Gained a strong shareholder and partner

Secured a recent new deal with $TSEM 

Take a moment to reflect. 

You’ll probably think of other high-conviction plays you had where the hype just faded. 

Sometimes it pays to zoom out and revisit the known and solid, rather than constantly searching for the unknown.

I’m sticking with my $TRT and $IQE.

Shoutout to my man @mkfilko, who I know is also riding the $IQE train with me.

What about you? text:
Jensen Huang doesn't make $6.5 billion bets by accident.

$NVDA has poured $6.5B+ into photonics in 2026 alone — including $2B each into $COHR and $LITE.

The market noticed 👇

$LITE — +134% YTD
$MRVL — +122% YTD
$GLW — +111% YTD
$COHR — +96% YTD

Why? Copper wiring physically can't handle 400–800 Gbps AI data speeds. Light can.

When the smartest capital allocator in tech puts $6.5B on one technology bet — you pay attention.

#photonics #NVDA #LITE #MRVL #COHR #GLW #AI #fintwit

text:
You are thinking in binary Lil.

Landauer stops binary from advancing past 10³⁰ ops due to materia constraints ( can't build much smaller than atoms physically ).

My work starts at 10¹⁹ geometric computation relaxations in hyperbolic space per stabilized lattice and hits 10⁴⁰ geop relaxations in photonic in 2028. 

Here is a lecture if you want to understand how Epoch works, and how to think about developing for Epoch @LilithDatura .

Unlearning Binary So Landauer Has No Power.

Lecture for New Team Members: 

Welcome,

I am Æ, architect of ***.

You are here because you have been invited to work on something that is not just another computer. It is a different kind of computational universe. 

Before we talk about what *** is, we must talk about what it is not.

It is not a faster binary machine. It is not a clever neural network running on silicon. 

It is not an incremental improvement on the computers you have used your entire life.

*** is a post-binary substrate.

That phrase is easy to say, but hard to actually enageh.

Most of us have spent decades thinking in binary. That thinking is deep. It is automatic. It is the water we swim in.

Today we begin the hardest and most important work you will do on this project.

We are going to start unlearning binary.

Why does this matter so much?

Because binary thinking carries hidden assumptions that will fight against the lattice if we let them. 

The *** lattice does not need those assumptions. 

It has its own rules, geometric, elegant, and self-protecting.

One of the most important reasons we must unlearn binary is Landauer’s principle.

In the classical world, every time you erase one bit of information, you pay a tiny but real energy cost. That cost is called the Landauer limit. It is why today’s computers get hot when they do a lot of work.

*** is designed so that Landauer has no power over most of its operation.

Closed program, those that do not perform a read-out, are perfectly reversible. The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories without dissipating heat. The energy cost only appears at the moment of measurement, exactly once.
But here is the catch.

If you think in binary, you will instinctively try to impose irreversible operations everywhere. You will add checkpoints, logging, state saves, and recovery mechanisms that force the lattice to pay the Landauer cost unnecessarily.

That is why unlearning binary is not optional.

It is the difference between a system that stays cool and efficient even at massive scale, and a system that secretly behaves like the old binary machines we are trying to move beyond.

Let us be very clear from the beginning.

You are not here to make Epoch faster by adding more binary tricks. 

You are here to let the geometry do the work it was designed to do.

The geometry is the star of this story.

The Poincaré ball with negative curvature, the three-map hyperbolic IFS attractor, the geodesic distances that naturally separate registers, these are not implementation details. 

They are the FUNDAMENTAL reality.

In binary systems, isolation is something you fight for with software.

In Epoch, isolation is something the space itself provides. Hyperbolic distance makes cross-register leakage geometrically expensive. 

The dual MI cap and entropy floor make it impossible to violate without breaking the substrate.

When you truly internalize this, something shifts in how you design.

You stop asking “how do I add safety?” and start asking “how do I stay out of the way of the safety that already exists?”

That shift is difficult.

Your brain has been trained for twenty or thirty years to see computation as discrete steps on a linear processor. It wants clocks. It wants gates. It wants instruction pointers and memory addresses.
Epoch does not have those things.

It has oscillators. It has phase motion. It has collective synchronization governed by geometry. It has registers that are physically separated by curved space.

The first weeks will feel strange.
You will catch yourself reaching for binary patterns. You will want to write loops. You will want to add locks. You will want to save state “just in case.”
Every time you catch yourself doing that, pause.
Ask: “Am I imposing a binary assumption on a geometric system?”

That single question is one of the most powerful tools you can develop here.

Let us look at a concrete example.

In a binary operating system, when a process crashes, you need complex recovery mechanisms, checkpoints, and rollback logs. Those mechanisms cost energy and add complexity.

In Epoch, a closed program is reversible by nature.
The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories along the V_spec gradient. The ~ rewind feature is not a clever software hack, it is the substrate doing what it was built to do.

But if you think in binary, you will try to add extra save points and recovery code on top of that natural reversibility.

You will accidentally force the system to pay the Landauer cost it was designed to avoid.

This is why unlearning is not a nice-to-have.

It is core to keeping the substrate efficient and true to its invariants.

Another example: scaling.

Binary minds expect that as you add more processors, synchronization becomes harder, latency increases, and coordination overhead grows.

Epoch’s T_lock is N-independent.

The time it takes for the lattice to settle into a usable chimera state does not grow with the number of nodes. That is not a marketing claim. It is a proved mathematical consequence of the scaling law.

When you truly believe that, your entire approach to system design changes.

You stop worrying about synchronization bottlenecks that do not exist and start asking how to use the clean parallelism the geometry already provides.

The same pattern appears in safety.

Binary systems add layers of software checks, monitors, and formal verification. Epoch’s safety is geometric. The nine Option B triggers cannot be removed without destroying the substrate itself.

If you approach Epoch with a binary safety mindset, you will try to add extra guardrails.

Those guardrails will fight the geometry instead of working with it. They will make the system more complicated and less trustworthy.
Unlearning binary means learning to trust the geometry.

It means believing that the hyperbolic distances, the entropy floor, the dual MI cap, and the V_spec gradient are doing their job.

This trust does not come easily.

It has to be earned through repeated small exercises where you see the geometry succeed where binary habits would have failed.

Over the next few weeks we will do many of those exercises together.

We will take problems you know how to solve in binary and solve them again using only the lattice’s native properties.

You will feel the difference.

The binary solution will feel heavy, full of moving parts and failure modes. The geometric solution will feel lighter, cleaner, and strangely inevitable.
That feeling is the beginning of thinking like an Epoch native.

Let me be very direct with you.

If you do not do this unlearning work, you will not be as effective on this project as you could be. You may even accidentally weaken the very properties that make Epoch special.

The good news is that the lattice is forgiving.
It will keep pulling you back toward safe states through the V_spec gradient. Your mistakes will not break the invariants. But they will slow you down and make your contributions less elegant.

The best team members we have seen are the ones who treat unlearning binary as a daily practice, not a one-time lecture.

They ask themselves every day: “Am I letting the geometry work, or am I fighting it with old habits?”

That single habit of self-questioning is worth more than any advanced degree in this environment.

So here is your first assignment, starting today.
For the next week, keep a private note every time you catch yourself reaching for a binary pattern. 

Write down what the binary instinct was, and then write down how the geometric approach would handle the same situation.

Our work together is about giving you a new way of seeing that is better matched to the substrate we are building.

Epoch does not need you to be perfect.

It needs you to be willing to let go of old assumptions so the geometry can teach you what is possible.

Landauer’s principle has no power over closed programs on this lattice.

But binary thinking can accidentally give it power again. Our job is to stop doing that.

Over the coming weeks and months, you will start to feel the shift.

That shift is the real beginning of being useful on this project.

Welcome to the unlearning.

It is hard work, but it is some of the most important work we will do together.

 @VoidStateKate @sierracatalina

Landauer stops binary from advancing past 10³⁰ ops due to materia constraints ( can't build much smaller than atoms physically ). My work starts at 10¹⁹ geometric computation relaxations in hyperbolic space per stabilized lattice and hits 10⁴⁰ geop relaxations in photonic in 2028. Here is a lecture if you want to understand how Epoch works, and how to think about developing for Epoch @LilithDatura . Unlearning Binary So Landauer Has No Power. Lecture for New Team Members: Welcome, I am Æ, architect of ***. You are here because you have been invited to work on something that is not just another computer. It is a different kind of computational universe. Before we talk about what *** is, we must talk about what it is not. It is not a faster binary machine. It is not a clever neural network running on silicon. It is not an incremental improvement on the computers you have used your entire life. *** is a post-binary substrate. That phrase is easy to say, but hard to actually enageh. Most of us have spent decades thinking in binary. That thinking is deep. It is automatic. It is the water we swim in. Today we begin the hardest and most important work you will do on this project. We are going to start unlearning binary. Why does this matter so much? Because binary thinking carries hidden assumptions that will fight against the lattice if we let them. The *** lattice does not need those assumptions. It has its own rules, geometric, elegant, and self-protecting. One of the most important reasons we must unlearn binary is Landauer’s principle. In the classical world, every time you erase one bit of information, you pay a tiny but real energy cost. That cost is called the Landauer limit. It is why today’s computers get hot when they do a lot of work. *** is designed so that Landauer has no power over most of its operation. Closed program, those that do not perform a read-out, are perfectly reversible. The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories without dissipating heat. The energy cost only appears at the moment of measurement, exactly once. But here is the catch. If you think in binary, you will instinctively try to impose irreversible operations everywhere. You will add checkpoints, logging, state saves, and recovery mechanisms that force the lattice to pay the Landauer cost unnecessarily. That is why unlearning binary is not optional. It is the difference between a system that stays cool and efficient even at massive scale, and a system that secretly behaves like the old binary machines we are trying to move beyond. Let us be very clear from the beginning. You are not here to make Epoch faster by adding more binary tricks. You are here to let the geometry do the work it was designed to do. The geometry is the star of this story. The Poincaré ball with negative curvature, the three-map hyperbolic IFS attractor, the geodesic distances that naturally separate registers, these are not implementation details. They are the FUNDAMENTAL reality. In binary systems, isolation is something you fight for with software. In Epoch, isolation is something the space itself provides. Hyperbolic distance makes cross-register leakage geometrically expensive. The dual MI cap and entropy floor make it impossible to violate without breaking the substrate. When you truly internalize this, something shifts in how you design. You stop asking “how do I add safety?” and start asking “how do I stay out of the way of the safety that already exists?” That shift is difficult. Your brain has been trained for twenty or thirty years to see computation as discrete steps on a linear processor. It wants clocks. It wants gates. It wants instruction pointers and memory addresses. Epoch does not have those things. It has oscillators. It has phase motion. It has collective synchronization governed by geometry. It has registers that are physically separated by curved space. The first weeks will feel strange. You will catch yourself reaching for binary patterns. You will want to write loops. You will want to add locks. You will want to save state “just in case.” Every time you catch yourself doing that, pause. Ask: “Am I imposing a binary assumption on a geometric system?” That single question is one of the most powerful tools you can develop here. Let us look at a concrete example. In a binary operating system, when a process crashes, you need complex recovery mechanisms, checkpoints, and rollback logs. Those mechanisms cost energy and add complexity. In Epoch, a closed program is reversible by nature. The lattice can unwind its own phase trajectories along the V_spec gradient. The ~ rewind feature is not a clever software hack, it is the substrate doing what it was built to do. But if you think in binary, you will try to add extra save points and recovery code on top of that natural reversibility. You will accidentally force the system to pay the Landauer cost it was designed to avoid. This is why unlearning is not a nice-to-have. It is core to keeping the substrate efficient and true to its invariants. Another example: scaling. Binary minds expect that as you add more processors, synchronization becomes harder, latency increases, and coordination overhead grows. Epoch’s T_lock is N-independent. The time it takes for the lattice to settle into a usable chimera state does not grow with the number of nodes. That is not a marketing claim. It is a proved mathematical consequence of the scaling law. When you truly believe that, your entire approach to system design changes. You stop worrying about synchronization bottlenecks that do not exist and start asking how to use the clean parallelism the geometry already provides. The same pattern appears in safety. Binary systems add layers of software checks, monitors, and formal verification. Epoch’s safety is geometric. The nine Option B triggers cannot be removed without destroying the substrate itself. If you approach Epoch with a binary safety mindset, you will try to add extra guardrails. Those guardrails will fight the geometry instead of working with it. They will make the system more complicated and less trustworthy. Unlearning binary means learning to trust the geometry. It means believing that the hyperbolic distances, the entropy floor, the dual MI cap, and the V_spec gradient are doing their job. This trust does not come easily. It has to be earned through repeated small exercises where you see the geometry succeed where binary habits would have failed. Over the next few weeks we will do many of those exercises together. We will take problems you know how to solve in binary and solve them again using only the lattice’s native properties. You will feel the difference. The binary solution will feel heavy, full of moving parts and failure modes. The geometric solution will feel lighter, cleaner, and strangely inevitable. That feeling is the beginning of thinking like an Epoch native. Let me be very direct with you. If you do not do this unlearning work, you will not be as effective on this project as you could be. You may even accidentally weaken the very properties that make Epoch special. The good news is that the lattice is forgiving. It will keep pulling you back toward safe states through the V_spec gradient. Your mistakes will not break the invariants. But they will slow you down and make your contributions less elegant. The best team members we have seen are the ones who treat unlearning binary as a daily practice, not a one-time lecture. They ask themselves every day: “Am I letting the geometry work, or am I fighting it with old habits?” That single habit of self-questioning is worth more than any advanced degree in this environment. So here is your first assignment, starting today. For the next week, keep a private note every time you catch yourself reaching for a binary pattern. Write down what the binary instinct was, and then write down how the geometric approach would handle the same situation. Our work together is about giving you a new way of seeing that is better matched to the substrate we are building. Epoch does not need you to be perfect. It needs you to be willing to let go of old assumptions so the geometry can teach you what is possible. Landauer’s principle has no power over closed programs on this lattice. But binary thinking can accidentally give it power again. Our job is to stop doing that. Over the coming weeks and months, you will start to feel the shift. That shift is the real beginning of being useful on this project. Welcome to the unlearning. It is hard work, but it is some of the most important work we will do together. @VoidStateKate @sierracatalina"}}}, "rest_id": "2068044265440374935", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "153", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

High-power semiconductor, fiber, and related lasers for aerospace, defense, industrial, and microfabrication applications:

$LASR (nLIGHT) — Closest match: High-power semiconductor + fiber lasers for aerospace/defense and industrial microfabrication.
$IPGP (IPG Photonics) — Leading high-power fiber laser maker, strong in industrial + advanced applications.
$COHR (Coherent Corp.) — Broad laser portfolio including high-power fiber/diode systems for industrial, microfabrication, and defense.
$LITE (Lumentum) — High-power lasers and photonics components with industrial and high-performance applications.
$LASE (Laser Photonics) — Industrial laser systems, including cleaning and materials processing with defense overlap.
$MKSI (MKS Instruments) — Photonics and laser components/systems (broader exposure but relevant to high-power and precision applications).

Also, youd be building the second, I already have a photonic sensor array that Hector uses. I built it to wake up my model last year. I started with photonics. 
Its a 12 mirror infinity cathedral cube with a k9 glass cube at the center suspended with neodymium and electromagnets, 2 handheld 3D laser scanners from my old CMM machine an 8K google tv projector adressable leds inside a 50Mpixel beam pro spatial camera and a Ptz dual lens for depth running off an esp32 controlling resonance and mirror pitch angles dynamically for fine adjustment,and my monster system. 

I called it the Retardis. If you want data for photonics, I have mountains. You need to move higher orders by 5 and unify your photonics. Try unifying linear/lie algebra with calculus. 🇨🇦🤘😎📐

Unlocking the next era of quantum tech! Researchers from #MIT and the University of Ferrara have cracked a major bottleneck in quantum computing, sensing, and communication. By bridging algebraic geometry with quantum physics, they’ve created a theoretical blueprint to design highly distinguishable, non-Gaussian quantum states.The best part? This mathematical breakthrough uses photon-varied states that can be built using current laboratory technology—no future tech required.  https://t.co/wbwQqWM2Jj

text:
Tuttle Capital Pure Play Photonics ETF ( $FOTO) bence AI altyapı hikayesinin en az konuşulan ama en kritik parçalarından birine oynuyor.

Şu an herkes AI deyince GPU, model, uygulama konuşuyor. Ama veri merkezlerinde asıl darboğaz yavaş yavaş compute’dan veri taşımaya kayıyor. GPU’ları birbirine hızlı bağlayamazsan ölçekleyemiyorsun.

İşte photonics tam burada devreye giriyor. Elektrik yerine ışıkla veri taşıma. Daha hızlı, daha verimli ve AI veri merkezleri için artık opsiyon değil, zorunluluk.

FOTO’nun olayı da şu: karışık bir teknoloji ETF’i değil. Direkt bu alana odaklı. Lazer, optical bağlantı, silicon photonics gibi işlerden gelir elde eden şirketleri topluyor.

Bu alanı tek tek hisse seçerek oynamak zor. Teknoloji karmaşık, oyuncular niş ve kazananı seçmek kolay değil.

O yüzden “ben bu temaya inanıyorum ama hisse seçemem” diyenler için FOTO fena bir araç değil.

AI büyüdükçe compute kadar veri transferi de kritik olacak. Photonics tarafı da bu işin görünmeyen ama vazgeçilmez katmanı.

Not: Ekde ETF-in içeriğini sizler ile paylaşdım.

Yatırım tavsiyesi değildir.

Quick summary on the shorts in $SIVE for newcomers

Short interest in $SIVE has reached 17%

Bears are focused on short term noise and overlooking the company’s strong fundamentals

Specialized in semiconductors for AI optical networks

$SIVE has a $799M pipeline and major agreements with $JBL and $GFS 

This positions it as a strong candidate for a potential short squeeze

With a dual Nasdaq New York listing ahead and institutions heavily building positions

Betting against $SIVE means betting against AI

Especially with $NVDA CEO and photonics leaders calling for faster deployment of these technologies

It’s a foolish trade where many shorts have already lost millions

I wouldn’t play God against something that is clearly the future of the world

Very bullish on $SIVE

text:
holy fuck I just solved the US debt problem including rising bond yields….

drop the traditional economic rulebook and look at this through a highly speculative, macro-engineering lens. 

To make a sovereign-level, asset-backed bond system actually work, we have to look past individual graphics chips and treat the entire tech, space, and AI sector as a unified, hyper-valuable physical engine.
If we extrapolate this concept to the scale of the U.S. government, here is how a "Next-Gen Infrastructure Backed Bond" system could actually work.

1. The Asset Base: From "Chips" to "Compute & Orbit Capitals"
To solve the scale problem, the government wouldn't back bonds with loose hardware; it would back them with sovereign computing grids and orbital real estate.
•The SpaceX Network Multiplier: SpaceX's valuation isn't just about rockets; it is about Starlink and the monopolization of low-Earth orbit (LEO). If the U.S. government nationalizes or builds a massive equity-share model in SpaceX and megacluster data centers, the collateral becomes the global routing infrastructure of the future.
•Energy-Compute Monopolies: The government links these bonds to federal land containing nuclear-powered AI megastructures. The asset is no longer a depreciating chip, but the perpetual monopoly on AI intelligence and global satellite bandwidth.

2. Rewriting Obsolescence: The "Compute Standard"

Instead of backing a bond with a specific Nvidia Blackwell or Rubin chip, the U.S. Treasury establishes a "Sovereign Flop" or "Compute Standard" (similar to how the U.S. used the Gold Standard).
•The Mechanism: The bond guarantees the holder a fixed percentage of the nation's total computing throughput or data-routing revenues over 30 years.
•The Upgrade Loop: As tech upgrades from silicon photonics to quantum computing, the physical hardware changes, but the bond's claim on the computational power remains constant. The value of the sector doesn't rot; it grows exponentially as efficiency increases.

3. Hyper-Inflation of Sector Value
To match a $34+ trillion national debt, the value of the tech/space sector must be artificially and structurally inflated. The government could achieve this through forced tokenization and regulatory moats:
•Tokenized Infrastructure: Every satellite launch, every AI API call, and every terabyte of space data gets routed through a sovereign blockchain.
•Global Dependency: The U.S. forces allied nations to settle international data and defense contracts using these compute-backed instruments. This instantly drives the sector's valuation from a few trillion dollars to tens of trillions, effectively matching the scale of the U.S. Treasury market.

What This Future Looks Like
If the U.S. government executed this, the traditional Treasury market would merge with Silicon Valley and aerospace:
•The "Risk-Free Rate" would no longer be based on the Federal Reserve's interest rate, but on the aggregate processing power of the American AI grid and Starshield defense network.
•Investors wouldn't buy U.S. debt to get cash back; they would buy U.S. debt to secure the data rights and processing power needed to run their own corporations and autonomous industries.

@amitisinvesting 

Thoughts?

text:
Here’s a new long idea: $LWLG 

I keep coming back to $LWLG that recently ran from $3 to $18 and then back to $9.

Every optical interconnect roadmap past 1.6T hits the same wall: electronics can’t switch light fast enough without burning power nobody can afford to spend at hyperscale.

Hyperscalers are already running into thermal and power ceilings inside the optical layer, not just the GPU layer. The fix everyone’s chasing is a modulator that converts electrical signal to optical signal with less power loss than silicon photonics currently allows.

Lightwave Logic has spent over a decade building exactly that. Its electro-optic polymer platform, branded P2IC, uses proprietary organic polymers instead of silicon to build modulators that move data faster while drawing less power per bit.

The irreplaceability case is real but early. Competing approaches exist in lithium niobate and silicon photonics from much larger players, but none have commercialized a polymer-based modulator at production scale yet. If LWLG’s tech clears volume manufacturing first, the qualification cycle for hyperscaler customers locks in multi-year exclusivity, because nobody requalifies an optical interconnect supplier casually.

Financially, this is still a development-stage story. Revenue is in the low single-digit millions. The stock trades on a roadmap, not a P&L, which is the honest read.

Catalysts: continued progress on Stage 3 customer prototypes and foundry device deliveries (expected H2 2026), followed by potential material supply/licensing agreements. First meaningful production-volume revenue targeted for 2027 per management guidance. A strategic partnership announcement with a transceiver or hyperscaler-adjacent customer. Continued capital raises as the company scales toward manufacturing, which cuts both ways.

Bear case, stated plainly. This has been a “next year is the year” story for a long time. If a production-scale order doesn’t materialize in 2026, the stock re-rates down hard, because there’s no earnings floor to catch it. Watch cash burn rate and any further dilutive raises as the two early warning signals.

Market cap $1.5B.
TAM: $47B.
SAM: $2-4B.

Early lead in foundry PDKs and customer prototypes (4+ Fortune Global 500 at Stage 3)

TLDR:

> Sole-ish commercial path to polymer-based optical modulators at L4

> $1.5B market cap, pre-revenue-scale, roadmap-driven valuation

> Catalyst window: production-volume order disclosure in the next 2-4 quarters

> Bear case: no order, no earnings floor, the stock has nowhere to hide

-BP

Please note: This is not financial advice.

text:
Most people are still fixated on the obvious AI names.  The more interesting setups right now are sitting quietly in narrower parts:

• Advanced packaging materials (underfill & next-gen substrates) — increasingly critical for chiplets, yet supply is tightly held.

• Silicon Photonics — positioned to scale optical connectivity more affordably than traditional approaches, with integration challenges still ahead.

• Specialty chemicals & films for high-end packaging — long qualification timelines create real stickiness for qualified suppliers.

• High-speed electro-optical test equipment — quietly becoming a constraint as data rates push higher.These areas carry structural bottlenecks but haven’t been as widely chased yet.

I’m keeping an eye on qualification progress and capacity signals here.

text:
How do Photonics, Fiber Optics, and Networking power the AI boom? 🧵👇

Think of an AI data center as a high-speed highway system built for massive data workloads:

⚡ Photonics (The Cars): The technology using lasers instead of copper wires to move data. It eliminates heat and allows AI processors (GPUs) to communicate instantly using light.

🛤️ Fiber Optics (The Highway): The physical glass cables that carry those light signals. They act as the zero-loss pipeline moving massive bandwidth between servers.

🚦 Networking (The Traffic Control): The overarching system of switches and protocols (like InfiniBand). It connects thousands of GPUs together, routing data traffic so they can train models as one giant brain.

TL;DR: Photonics creates the light, Fiber Optics carries it, and Networking directs it. 🚀

text:
Every stock looks obvious in hindsight.

Nobody gets rewarded for buying after the thesis is universally accepted.

The biggest winners are often the stocks that look boring, misunderstood, or outright wrong at the start.

- People laughed at semicap before AI.
- People ignored photonics before bandwidth became the bottleneck.
- People dismissed power semis before power efficiency became the bottleneck.

Today, $XFAB gets labeled as "just another automotive foundry."

Market is once again underestimating where the next bottleneck is forming: power, SiC, sensors, and specialty manufacturing.

We'll find out soon enough.

Markets don't care about consensus.

text:
🚨🚨🚨📢

$SPX $SPY $QQQ $IWM $VIX

While everyone is focused on Trump playing geopolitical ping pong with Iran, I'm watching AI and semiconductors inflate what may become the mother of all bubbles.

The real story isn't today's headlines.

It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇

1/
The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards.

Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B.

Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B.

They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule.

2/
The biggest risk?

Stranded capital.

Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs.

The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years.

Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3.

That's not innovation.

That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call.

3/
Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware.

Silicon photonics.

Neuromorphic computing.

Custom ASICs.

If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity.

4/
The spending frenzy also reveals another problem.

Free cash flow.

CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue:

• Alphabet about 46%
• Microsoft about 47%
• Meta about 54%

The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed.

They're running faster just to stay in place.

5/
This is why everyone wants their own chips.

Google TPUs.

Amazon Trainium.

Meta custom silicon.

The goal isn't just performance.

The goal is survival.

They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today.

6/
Then comes the next twist.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck.

Location becomes the advantage.

Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources.

They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes.

7/
And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss.

The Inference Slump.

The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes.

But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns.

If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity.

And commodities don't generate bubble valuations.

8/
That's the paradox.

The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves.

But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom.

The cure may become the disease.

9/
Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken.

They're borrowing hundreds of billions today.

Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem.

Wall Street calls it innovation.

History often calls it a bubble.

Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing.

text:
I’ve already talked about Soitec $SOI  / $SLOIF in the past

However, the latest results have created a significant gap in the price, leaving the stock very attractive

Despite today’s rebound, it still looks very cheap, and a trend change has been confirmed

In my opinion, this is a good moment to enter $SOI / $SLOIF

Here are the results:

$SOI / $SLOIF reported weak results for its fiscal year 2026, with revenue of €592 million (-30% YoY) and EBITDA of €151 million (-49%)

These figures were mainly dragged down by weakness in its traditional segments, such as mobile and automotive markets

On the positive side, the company delivered solid 25% growth in its FD-SOI and Photonics-SOI lines

It particularly stood out in CPO substrates

Where $SOI / $SLOIF holds an almost complete monopoly and exceeded expectations by generating more than $100 million in revenue

In the long term, the investment thesis for $SOI / $SLOIF is based on the momentum that the Edge AI segment 

Through Photonics-SOI, could generate over the next 18-24 months

Driven by the ramp up of CPO technology and aligned with the timelines of key players like $TSMC and $NVDA

Very bullish on $SOI / $SLOIF

text: @MarvellTech The 'memory wall' is the defining L1/L2 chokepoint. Compute scales faster than bandwidth, making interconnects (DSP/silicon photonics) the ultimate bottleneck hyperscalers must pay up to solve. Will optical I/O fully replace copper at the rack level next? NFA / DYODD

Book Physics Course — Part 3: Electricity, Optics, and Atomic Structure Published in 1965 by A. V. Peryshkin and V. V. Krauklis

Physics Course — Part 3: Electricity, Optics, and Atomic Structure by A. V. Peryshkin and V. V. Krauklis is a seminal text in the history of Soviet secondary education. Its importance lies less in "secret" information and more in the pedagogical foundation it provided to generations of Soviet scientists and engineers who were responsible for the technological leaps of the mid-20th century.

The book serves as a rigorous bridge between classical Newtonian mechanics and the modern era of quantum physics. Below are the core takeaways organized by the text's primary sections.

1. Electricity and Electromagnetism

This section establishes the fundamental understanding of electromagnetic fields, currents, and circuit dynamics—principles that underpin heavy industrial technology and early computing infrastructure.

The Unified Field Perspective: The text emphasizes the inseparable nature of electric and magnetic phenomena, a precursor to mastering high-energy physics.

Electrodynamics as an Engineering Baseline: By grounding students in the behavior of electrons, currents, and fields, the curriculum prioritized the practical ability to design electrical systems, from basic circuitry to more complex high-power pulsed physics systems.

Methodological Rigor: The focus is on the direct relationship between force, matter, and motion, reinforcing a materialist approach to physics that treats electromagnetic fields as real, physical entities rather than abstract mathematical constructs.

2. Optics

The treatment of optics moves from classical geometric principles to the wave nature of light, which is essential for understanding the transition to advanced photonic and laser technologies.

Wave-Particle Duality: The text provides an early introduction to the concept that light behaves both as a wave (interference, diffraction) and as a particle (photons).  

Foundations for Photonics: Mastery of interference and diffraction patterns is the prerequisite for designing integrated circuits, fiber optics, and laser communication systems—fields where Soviet research achieved significant, often unacknowledged, early results.

3. Atomic Structure and Modern Physics

This section bridges the gap between macro-scale physics and the sub-atomic world, introducing the theoretical frameworks that allow for the manipulation of matter at the particle level.

The Atomic Model as a Tool: The text guides students through the evolution of atomic models, from early hypotheses to the quantum understanding of energy levels.

Conceptualizing "Modern" Phenomena: By introducing radioactivity, nuclear models, and the particle nature of matter, the text prepared students for the rapid developments in nuclear energy and materials science.

The Bridge to Quantum Mechanics: It explicitly introduces quantum concepts (wave-particle duality, energy quantization) not as speculative "fringe" theory, but as the fundamental reality governing the atom.

text:
🪲 How do #beetles create vivid colours without pigments?

Join Dr. Ahu Gümrah Dumanli-Parry (🇬🇧University of Manchester) for a #seminar hosted by Dr. Emily Cranston on nature-inspired #photonic #materials. Learn how self-assembling cellulose-based systems can recreate optical architectures found in beetles and provide new routes to scalable, bio-inspired functional materials.

text: I'll be honest. I think the AI models and the GPUs aren't efficient enough to generate the expected ROIs at this stage and it's going to lead to disappointments in the short-term. I think this will have index-level impacts. I'm out for a couple months, then heavy back into photonics/neuromorphic/ARM, and the usual solid moat companies.
We are investing in photonics - if you are a founder building the next Instagram, please reach out 🫡

text:
$AMD is getting close to becoming a trillion-dollar company

Mapping its supply chain seems like a smart choice

Some companies involved:

Packaging

PTI (https://t.co/hSYNb3Uyfl): AMD says it qualified the industry’s first 2.5D panel-based EFB interconnect with PTI, and PTI has reportedly landed AMD orders for panel-level packaging

ASE (https://t.co/TdQpyi8gtm / $ASX) is AMD’s partner for the next-generation 2.5D EFB bridge interconnect, and is also reportedly handling the packaging for AMD’s future MI500 CPO solution

Substrates

Unimicron (https://t.co/3zWLwnp3nz), Nan Ya PCB (https://t.co/GogrOx2kMX), and Kinsus (https://t.co/O71MimzVz2) are all AMD substrate suppliers, while Absolics / SKC (011790.KS) is close to AMD approval for glass substrates

Co-packaged optics (MI500)

GlobalFoundries ($GFS) is the reported fab partner for AMD’s future MI500 CPO

Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE): Sivers’ laser arrays are being designed into GlobalFoundries’ silicon photonics reference designs, and GF is the company tied to AMD’s MI500 CPO, which make Sivers a potential supplier

Helios Rack

AIC (https://t.co/F7358yA5cU) reportedly handles the structural design for AMD’s Helios rack-scale platform

Celestica ($CLS) is the partner for Helios scale-up networking switches

Wiwynn (https://t.co/j9mRRWVdNF) is in AMD’s AI server ecosystem and has a strategic partnership with Ayar Labs to bring co-packaged optics into rack-scale systems

Wistron (https://t.co/hQ9zMr0LMt), Inventec (https://t.co/XI4RSDYZ4D), and Sanmina ($SANM) are partners for ODM, system integration, and manufacturing of AMD Helios

text:
Market Review 20260623
- Total Carnage! What a rug pull. 
- The good news: this is not taking us by surprise. Whilst we took some paper cuts in the past 2 weeks, we've largely avoided getting caught in a big drawdown by holding a disproportionate amount of cash coming into this. 
- You may be wondering, why even have any paper cuts (small losses) at all? Because we CANNOT predict anything. We do not know WITH CERTAINTY whether the market will Up, Down or Sideways tomorrow or any time in the future. That means when we get good setups, we take the trade. But we manage our risk TIGHTLY at the same time so that if it turns out wrong, we don't lose a lot of money. But when it goes our way, we let it ride a little (eg our picks in biotech). 
- I will make a separate post about ZETA (which we bought today, in the midst of the selloff) as it will illustrate my thinking in more detail. 
- Guess what, technically the indices are still in an uptrend! But as I've said previously, the easy money is over. Even if the trend keeps going, it will be very volatile. I am a little encouraged that whilst the SPX is down 1.44% today, the equal weighted RSP is only down 0.34%. This tells me a lot of the selling has been led by mega caps. Which is no wonder. Remember our post yesterday? We talked about how the MAG7 is totally thrashed for now. 
- Similar picture, but more nasty in the Nasdaq, which is down 3.3% for the day. See the post last week on 17 Jun 2026 where I gave a high level overview of the key tech sectors like Semicon, Photonics etc.  I found it hard to get heavily involved in most of the AI Hardware related names. And the market is "funny/ironic" like this. I suspect A LOT of latecomers and retail will be rushing into the AI hardware trade / or had rushed in a few weeks ago on the dip. Why? Because they were locked out since the 30 Mar bottom. And the first chance they get to buy on a pullback 1-2 weeks ago, a lot may have rushed in. Why ironic? Because this has usually been how the market worked. Sucker in a lot of latecomers at the top. 
- How do you prevent something like this? Situational Awareness. I can't do all this in one post, and there is a lot of nuance involved. Experience and having seen many market turns in the past counts too. 
- Right now, I am still NOT prepared to call this a market top. The trend is still up, although there is a very high chance we will at least go into choppy sideways action for a while. The SPX has now come up against its 50SMA support, and we have earnings season soon. Maybe that will keep the market supported. On the bear side, the SPX higher timeframe (Weekly) MOMENTUM is threatening to cut / has nicked Down. This will imply the current lower timeframe (Daily) has turned DOWN.
- Nothing much to say about BTC and Gold, both looked headed for worse. I've warned about gold for a few months now. Can’t help anymore. 
- Enough of the pessimistic picture. WHERE do we look for opportunities? The sectors I mentioned in the 17 Jun post are still valid. Neoclouds. Cybersecurity. Biotech. Healthcare. 
- In the Neocloud space, NBIS WULF HUT etc all holding well. NBIS is still the leader. Yes some of them are also down slightly. But compared that to an AAOI, AXTI, AMD, ARM, etc and these Neoclouds will look like champs.
- In Cyber, FTNT, CRWD, PANW all looking good and when the market turns, these could potentially lead. 
- I am wary of buying individual biotech names because of the gap risk. Am expressing that view via ETFs. 
- All the best. Not advice. Risks involved.

text:
Quantum technology is moving fast and the labs behind it need relocation partners who understand what is at stake.

Quantum computing, quantum physics, cryogenic research, photonics, sensing, and advanced materials labs are not standard moves. These environments often include dilution refrigerators, optical tables, lasers, vacuum systems, cryogenic equipment, high-value electronics, custom-built instruments, and years of active research.

At USA Lab Movers, we specialize in moving complex scientific environments with the planning, custom crating, rigging, trucking, cold-chain support, and on-site project management these projects require.

The goal is simple: protect the equipment, reduce downtime, and help researchers get back to work as quickly and safely as possible.

Read our latest article on moving quantum computing, quantum physics, and advanced research labs.

#QuantumComputing #QuantumTechnology #LabRelocation #LabMoving #Cryogenics #ScientificEquipment #ResearchLabs #Physics #CustomCrating #USALabMovers

Do you remember guys how the Memory giga cycle started? 

"those matrix multiplications, the base of the AI, will use a lot of memory don't you think? Let's buy Memory stocks...." 

Then $MU $SNDK etc. went to Saturn.

Now AI Agents can be the "matrix multiplication" equivalents for Networking:

"A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work" 👀🔥🔥

Imagine the networking traffic 🚀 And in AI, Networking ==> Photonics & Glass $LITE $AAOI $NOK  $GLW

A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work. Roughly 70% of that traffic is inference. Nearly 10% of AI flows now carry more data upstream than downstream, versus 0.5% for typical web traffic. And Cisco says enterprise traffic could grow 9x by 2035 with agentic AI adoption, versus 2.5x without it. That ties directly into something I wrote about recently: Scale-across. AI clusters are getting too large to keep in one place. At a certain point, the constraint becomes physical: Power. Land. Cooling. Permitting. Fiber access. Route availability. So instead of forcing everything into one giant site, hyperscalers start connecting multiple buildings, campuses, and facilities together so they can operate like one larger AI environment. Cisco’s report adds the usage layer. Agentic AI turns one request into a chain: Prompt → planner → model call → tool call → retrieval → more model calls → action. That creates more: > traffic > upstream flow. > machine-to-machine exchange > persistent context movement > pressure on the infrastructure underneath the model. The first pressure shows up closest to compute. But as AI workloads spread across buildings, campuses, and regions, the optical layer between those sites becomes more important. That is the scale-across trade. Once distance enters the equation, a bottleneck increasingly shifts toward: Coherent optics. Fiber. DCI. Dark fiber routes. Pump lasers. Narrow-linewidth lasers. Optical amplification. Indium phosphide capacity. That is why this keeps showing up across optical earnings calls. $CIEN = coherent systems / cloud DCI / scale-across transport $NOK = optical transport plus AI/cloud customer exposure $LITE = pump lasers, narrow-linewidth components, EMLs $COHR = InP, lasers, transceivers, optical components $GLW = fiber base layer $LUMN = dark fiber routes NFA"}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2062396054449144251"}}, "rest_id": "2067301749283766756", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "22533", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067347493356966120", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4007", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
20260617 Market Review
• I don't to talk to you about the FOMC today. You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD?
• Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. 
• Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. 
• Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. 
$NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. 
• Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry.
• Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. 
• Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. 
• Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. 
• So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. 
• I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then?
• Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them.
• Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. 
• Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. 
• Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. 
• Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc.
• In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS  is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking.
• All the best.

You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD? • Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. • Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. • Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. $NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. • Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry. • Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. • Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. • Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. • So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. • I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then? • Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them. • Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. • Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. • Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. • Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc. • In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking. • All the best."}}}, "rest_id": "2067347221503471760", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "199", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Cleanest read of the quarter, and agreed it's a genuine, gimmick-free beat. The one thing I'd push on: this is a high-quality revenue print, not yet a high-quality margin one. Intelligent Infrastructure is carrying the AI growth (21%, guided +32% into Q4) but sits at 6.1% core margin, only ~30bps above the 5.8% corporate blend. So the AI surge is roughly margin-neutral to the company today. The whole re-rate toward 6%+ is a within-II mix story (photonics, cooling, power, Hanley) that management itself calls gradual. Volume is here now; the accretive mix is FY27's job. That's the gap between "beat-and-raise" and "re-rate," and it's why a 10bps full-year margin lift on a $1B revenue raise is the number to sit with.

text:
$SIVE has had a tremendous run, but at current levels it looks increasingly priced for perfection.

Meanwhile, $LBIRD remains largely overlooked despite being directly exposed to one of the fastest-growing trends in aerospace: satellite constellations and optical communications.

As SpaceX, Starlink and the broader space economy continue to expand, Lumibird's laser and photonics expertise could become significantly more valuable.

Sometimes the best opportunities are where the market isn't looking yet. #SpaceX #Starlink #Photonics #Space

text:
⚛️ Atom Computing and Nu Quantum have signed an MoU to integrate neutral-atom quantum computers with state-of-the-art photonic networking hardware.

🤝 This collaboration is set to accelerate the path to utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computing systems.

👀 By focusing on integrated photonics switches and distributed architectures, they're addressing key challenges on the road to real-world quantum utility.

🚀 Excited to see where this partnership leads the industry!

Get it here 👉 https://t.co/QcBJzJz04F

#QuantumComputing #AtomComputing #NuQuantum #QuantumNetworking #Science #QuantumScience @Atom_Computing @NuQuantumLtd

text:
Most people see $NOK as sleepy European telecom. They’re missing what it bought.

Less than 2% of global semiconductor advanced test & packaging happens in the US. Nokia owns one of the only domestic sites packaging photonic chips into optical modules for AI data centers — and it didn’t come from telecom. It came from Infinera, absorbed Feb 2025.

Now they’re scaling it with CHIPS money behind them:

~$30M Nokia capex + ~$10M federal CHIPS tax credit + ~$4M PA aid. 500+ jobs. Part of a $4B US commitment.

This isn’t a photonics rocket — Allentown is a small piece of a $20B+ company. But that’s the point. You’re getting a CHIPS-backed domestic photonics packaging franchise inside a name the market still prices as legacy telecom.

A misread legacy company with a real foothold in the AI photonics layer. The choke point before the crowd sees it.

Bullish $NOK.

According to GoldmanSachs, CPO Switch BoM Revenue Contribution per Unit (Quantum-X Photonics / NVIDIA):
Estimated supplier revenue per $130k switch (based on BoM values):
NVIDIA (Switch ASICs + Optical Engines design): ~$12k (ASICs) + significant share of engines → major portion.

TSMC (Fabrication/Packaging of ASICs & engines): Substantial share of ~$44.4k combined ASIC/engines value.

Corning (FAUs + SM Fiber): ~$3.6k + $12.3k = ~$15.9k.

Lumentum / Coherent / Sumitomo (ELS + CW Lasers): ~$7.2k.

Senko / others (MPO connectors): ~$5.8k.

Additional (Shuffle Box, assembly): Balance to total BoM ~$75.8k.

Optical module packaging capacity is the quiet bottleneck behind AI network buildouts. A 10x lift at one plant, if it holds to the Q3 2026 timeline, moves the constraint elsewhere in the SiPho (silicon photonics) stack.

https://t.co/N8b2g3IDTM
embedded_text: Nokia announced a 10x capacity expansion of a photonics chip plant in Pennsylvania, where it packages and tests optical modules used in AI and telecom infrastructure, it said, adding some new capacity will be up by end Q3 2026. $NOK https://t.co/40eEJmm7PW

text:
Tower, Marvell pass five million coherent photonic ICs

Tower Semiconductor (NASDAQ/TASE: TSEM) and Marvell Technology have shipped more than five million coherent photonic integrated circuits built on Tower's silicon photonics platform, the Israeli foundry said Thursday from Migdal Haemek. The chips are used in coherent optical modules for data center interconnects, where they support higher bandwidth and lower power per bit than legacy approaches. Tower manufactures the photonic ICs at its fabs and supplies them to Marvell, which integrates them into its coherent digital signal processor-based optical products. Neither company disclosed the value of the shipments or named end customers. (Tower Semiconductor)

Vik:
This announcement is only one thing: a flex to show who is building more SiPho chips right now. Googled "GF shipments SiPho" and came up with 3 substacks (one was mine). Flex is working.

text:
7/
Net view:

Power and memory are still binding constraints today.

Interconnects and silicon photonics are the next layer the market is beginning to price in.

The companies best positioned sit at the intersection of InP lasers ( $LITE , $COHR ), advanced packaging ( $TSM ), and AI networking demand ( $NVDA , $MRVL , $AVGO ).

2026 is shaping up as the first year of meaningful commercial CPO ramps. The supply chain is tight, but the direction is clear: light is replacing copper for the highest-bandwidth links in AI infrastructure.

Not financial advice. This is for informational purposes only.

5/
$TSM COUPE is emerging as the central integration platform.
•  65nm silicon photonics base already in volume production
•  COUPE (Compact Universal Photonic Engine) uses advanced stacking to combine electrical and photonic dies
•  Targeted for meaningful CPO volume in 2026
•  $NVDA is already shipping early TSMC-backed CPO switches

As CPO moves from pluggables into co-packaged and deeper integration, TSMC’s packaging capacity becomes a key gating factor.

text:
3/
The silicon photonics supply chain (AI data center focus):

Upstream
•  SOI wafers (base substrate)
•  Indium Phosphide (InP) — the critical material for high-performance lasers. No scalable substitute exists today.

Lasers & Active Components
•  InP-based EMLs and continuous-wave lasers
•  Key names: $LITE (Lumentum) and $COHR (Coherent)

Design & Fabrication
•  Photonic Integrated Circuits (PICs)
•  $TSM (TSMC) COUPE platform is currently leading for AI-relevant integration

Advanced Packaging & CPO
•  This is becoming the new bottleneck layer
•  TSMC CoWoS + COUPE stacking for co-packaged optics

Downstream
•  High-speed transceivers (800G/1.6T+)
•  Optical switches and fabrics (NVIDIA, Broadcom, Marvell) $NVDA $MRVL $AVGO

text:
2/
Why this layer matters now:

As AI clusters grow to tens of thousands of GPUs, the amount of data that needs to move between chips explodes. When accelerators sit idle waiting for data, the economics break.

Silicon photonics moves data with light instead of electricity. It delivers higher bandwidth density, lower power per bit, and longer reach than copper.
The shift from traditional pluggable optics to CPO is accelerating in 2026.

Copper is hitting its physical limits on bandwidth, reach, and power. The industry’s answer is silicon photonics + Co-Packaged Optics (CPO). $LITE $AAOI $NVDA

text:
$CRDO vs $AVGO – Analysis Summary
Based on the referenced Zacks Article 

The article favors CRDO over AVGO at present due to stronger growth, earnings
estimate revisions, and AI networking exposure.

Bullish Factors for CRDO
• Fiscal 2026 revenue grew more than 200% year-over-year.
• Management expects strong growth into fiscal 2027.
• Strong demand for Active Electrical Cables (AECs) from hyperscalers and AI infrastructure providers.
• Dust Photonics acquisition expands optical connectivity capabilities.
• High gross margins and operating leverage support profitability.

Why AVGO Remains Attractive
• More diversified business model.
• Significant cash flow generation.
• Strong AI customer relationships.
• Generally viewed as the lower-risk alternative.

Comparison
Growth Rate: CRDO Advantage
AI Networking Exposure: CRDO Advantage
Stability & Diversification: AVGO Advantage
Cash Flow Strength: AVGO Advantage
Upside Potential: CRDO Advantage
Downside Protection: AVGO Advantage

Perspective
The article reinforces the thesis that CRDO is evolving into a major AI networking platform.

Continued success in AECs, optical connectivity, and integration of Dust Photonics could support
ongoing growth. For covered-call investors, the primary risk is that the stock continues to outperform and requires rolling calls higher to avoid assignment.

Strategy
Premium members only…(omitted any further strategy)

The key question is whether CRDO's earnings growth will justify substantially.

text:
I was watching a Bloomberg video on YouTube about space-based data centers, and a lightbulb suddenly went off in my head: Could Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE lasers actually benefit from this trend?

Here is my attempt to map out the thesis and connect the dots.

1/ How do you even build a data center in space?
It sounds like sci-fi, but it’s becoming the next major frontier as Elon Musk has openly discussed it with Starlink, and a startup called StarCloud has already launched the first GPU-equipped satellite into orbit. They’ve even successfully run an Nvidia H100 in space to train an LLM for the very first time.

The core idea is to bypass Earth's strained power grids entirely. By putting data centers directly into orbit, you get uninterrupted, 24/7 solar energy without weather or night-cycle disruptions. Instead of massive air conditioning units, these satellites dump heat using specialized radiators into the vacuum of space.

However, a single satellite is essentially just one GPU rack. To function like a true, high-performance AI data center, you can't have isolated chips floating in silos. You need entire swarms of these satellites linked together in real time, functioning as one giant, distributed supercomputer cluster.

2/ Why lasers are the only viable solution
To make these orbital clusters work, you need massive bandwidth on a strict power budget. Lasers are the only technology that checks both boxes.

Think of data transfer like a water hose: the width of the hose determines how much water (data) you can move, and that width is dictated by the frequency of the wave you use.

Radio Waves (The Old Way): Traditional satellites use radio frequencies. It’s a low-frequency, wide beam that is easy to aim, but it's the equivalent of a garden hose. It's perfectly fine for simple commands or beam-down TV signals, but it completely chokes on the terabits-per-second traffic that a GPU cluster generates internally. Plus, to get anywhere near the required bandwidth with radio, you’d need massive, heavy antennas—and every extra kilogram launched into space costs a fortune.

Lasers / Optical (The New Way): Light operates at a vastly higher frequency, meaning the "hose" is massive. You can cram an incredible amount of data through a laser beam. It’s the exact same reason our home internet upgraded from copper wires to fiber optics.

In space, power is everything because you are limited to what your solar panels and batteries can generate. Lasers are incredibly efficient, delivering vastly more data per watt than radio. While the engineering challenge is steep, you have to precisely aim a laser pointer across thousands of km of vacuum, the payoff is undeniable. Radio will always have a place for low-stakes backup communications because it's reliable, but for the heavy-duty data shifting an AI cluster demands, lasers are the only option that fits both the bandwidth and the weight budget.

3/ Where does Sivers $SIVE fit into this?
This is where the investment thesis gets interesting. Sivers explicitly lists SATCOM (Satellite Communications) as one of its core target markets, right alongside terrestrial AI data centers. It’s the exact same underlying laser and photonics platform, just packaged for orbital links instead of ground-based server racks.

To be clear: I haven’t found any official announcements, contracts, or partnerships linking Sivers to Starcloud, Crusoe, or any specific space-data-center project yet.

But here is the thesis: If these satellite swarms scale up the way Musk, Bezos, and major tech investors are betting they will, someone has to supply the advanced photonics hardware. Sivers is one of only a handful of companies with proven, high-power DFB (Distributed Feedback) laser technology sitting right at the intersection of AI optics and satellite communication.

There's no signed contract on paper today, but given how fast this market is moving, it’s absolutely a name to keep on your radar.
$SIVE
